topics.title: Design and Analysis of RC Coupled Amplifier, Need for Different Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCPL307 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL ELECTRONICS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Design and Analysis of RC Coupled Amplifier, Need for Different Components # Module Experiment 2: Design and Analysis of RC Coupled Amplifier ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **RC Coupled Amplifier (Definition):** An **RC Coupled Amplifier** is a multi-stage small-signal voltage amplifier in which the output of one Common Emitter (CE) transistor stage is coupled to the input of the next stage through a **Resistor-Capacitor (RC) network**. The coupling capacitor blocks the DC bias of one stage from interfering with the DC bias of the subsequent stage, while the resistor provides the DC path and sets the input impedance for the next stage. This topology is the **workhorse of audio-frequency (AF) amplification** and forms the fundamental building block of virtually every commercial audio amplifier, pre-amplifier, and intermediate frequency strip in communication receivers. In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PCCPL307 – Analog and Digital Electronics Lab**, this experiment is classified under **Module 2: BJT and FET Circuits**, with the following expected Course Outcomes (COs): - **CO2:** Design and analyze BJT/FET-based amplifier circuits and evaluate their performance parameters. - **CO3:** Examine the frequency response and signal-conditioning behavior of multi-stage amplifiers. - **CO4:** Interpret experimental observations and correlate them with theoretical derivations. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The Bucket-Brigade Fire Line:** > Imagine a line of people (transistor stages) passing buckets of water (AC signal) down a firebreak. Each person can only lift a small bucket (low gain per stage), so to move a large volume, several people must pass the bucket sequentially. > - The **person's arms** are the **transistor (active device)** providing the energy to lift the next bucket. > - The **coupling capacitor** is like a **sealed lid on the bucket** — it lets the *water* (AC signal) pass but prevents *sludge* (DC bias voltage) from one person contaminating the next person's grip. > - The **coupling/load resistor** is the **rigid handle** that gives the next person something firm to grab onto (sets impedance and develops the output voltage). > - The **emitter bypass capacitor** is like a **shortcut pipe** that lets the AC signal bypass the emitter resistor (which would otherwise reduce the amplification effort). ### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Design Metrics for an RC Coupled Amplifier:** > - **Mid-band Frequency Range:** **20 Hz to 20 kHz** (audible range, $f_L \approx 50$ Hz, $f_H \approx 20$ kHz for cascaded stages) > - **Thermal Voltage:** $V_T = \frac{kT}{q} \approx \mathbf{26\ mV}$ at $T = 300$ K > - **BJT Intrinsic Emitter Resistance:** $r_e' = \frac{V_T}{I_E} = \frac{26\ \text{mV}}{I_E\ (\text{mA})}$ in **ohms** > - **BJT Small-Signal Resistance:** $r_\pi = (\beta+1) r_e' \approx \beta \cdot r_e'$ > - **Standard Silicon $V_{BE}$:** **0.7 V** (active region bias) > - **Typical Supply Voltage $V_{CC}$:** **12 V** or **±12 V** --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Block Diagram of a Two-Stage RC Coupled Amplifier A two-stage RC coupled amplifier consists of two identical CE stages cascaded using coupling networks. The first stage amplifies the weak input signal, and the second stage further amplifies the output of the first stage, producing a much larger final output. **Signal Flow:** $$\text{Input } (V_{in}) \rightarrow \text{Stage 1 (CE)} \rightarrow \text{Coupling Network (C_C, R)} \rightarrow \text{Stage 2 (CE)} \rightarrow \text{Output } (V_{out})$$ ### 2.2 Need for Each Component — Detailed Functional Justification > [!NOTE] > **Why each component is required is the most frequently asked conceptual question (3 marks) in KTU university exams.** Memorize the function of every part. | # | Component | Functional Need | Consequence of Removal | |---|-----------|-----------------|------------------------| | 1 | **Coupling Capacitor $C_C$ (Input)** | Blocks DC from source reaching the bias network; passes only AC signal into the base. | DC offset from the source would shift the Q-point and drive the transistor out of the active region. | | 2 | **Bias Resistors $R_1$, $R_2$ (Voltage Divider)** | Establish a stable DC base voltage to fix the Q-point in the active region. | Transistor would saturate (no $V_{CE}$ swing) or cut-off (no current). | | 3 | **Emitter Resistor $R_E$** | Provides DC stability against $\beta$ variations and temperature changes (swamping). | Q-point would drift with temperature, leading to thermal runaway. | | 4 | **Emitter Bypass Capacitor $C_E$** | Bypasses $R_E$ for AC signals, restoring the high voltage gain that $R_E$ reduces at DC. | Voltage gain $A_v$ would drop drastically (from ~100 to ~5–10). | | 5 | **Collector Resistor $R_C$** | Converts collector current variations into a voltage output (acts as a load for the transistor). | No voltage amplification — output would equal input (gain $\approx 1$). | | 6 | **Coupling Capacitor $C_{C2}$ (Inter-stage)** | Blocks DC of stage-1 collector from reaching stage-2 base; couples AC. | DC bias of stage 1 would disturb stage 2 bias; both stages would malfunction. | | 7 | **Coupling Resistor $R$ (Inter-stage)** | Provides a defined AC path and a high input impedance to the next stage so that loading effect is minimized. | Severe loading effect; overall gain collapses. | | 8 | **Bypass Capacitor across $V_{CC}$ ($C_{BYP}$)** | Filters out AC ripple from the power supply. | Hum and noise from the supply would appear at the output. | | 9 | **BJT (Q1, Q2)** | Active device providing the power gain; controlled current source. | No amplification possible. | ### 2.3 Small-Signal AC Equivalent Circuit Analysis To analyze the amplifier's behavior at mid-band frequencies, we replace the coupling and bypass capacitors with **short circuits** (since their reactance is negligible at mid-band) and the DC supply $V_{CC}$ with a **ground** (AC ground). **Key Mid-Band Voltage Gain of a Single CE Stage (with $C_E$ fully bypassing $R_E$):** $$A_v = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = -\frac{\beta \cdot (R_C \parallel R_L \parallel r_o)}{r_\pi}$$ For $r_o \to \infty$ and approximating the loading: $$A_v \approx -\frac{R_C'}{r_e'} = -\frac{R_C \parallel R_L}{r_e'}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > The negative sign indicates a **180° phase reversal** between input and output — a defining feature of a common-emitter amplifier and a high-yield KTU viva question. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!NOTE] > The following table compiles every formula you need to solve any RC coupled amplifier problem in the lab exam. Note the use of `\vert` instead of `|` to protect markdown table integrity. | # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Boundary / Note | |---|-----------|---------|-------|-----------------| | 1 | Thermal Voltage | $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q}$ | V | **26 mV** at 300 K | | 2 | Intrinsic Emitter Resistance | $r_e' = \dfrac{V_T}{I_E} = \dfrac{26\ \text{mV}}{I_E\ (\text{mA})}$ | $\Omega$ | Always in ohms | | 3 | Base-Emitter Dynamic Resistance | $r_\pi = (\beta+1)\, r_e' \approx \beta \cdot r_e'$ | $\Omega$ | — | | 4 | Mid-band Voltage Gain (CE) | $A_v = -\dfrac{R_C \parallel R_L}{r_e'}$ | dimensionless | Negative sign $\Rightarrow$ 180° phase shift | | 5 | Overall Gain (Two-Stage) | $A_{v,\text{total}} = A_{v1} \cdot A_{v2}$ | dimensionless | Product, not sum | | 6 | Gain in Decibels (dB) | $A_v(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10} \vert A_v \vert$ | dB | Always positive in dB | | 7 | Lower Cut-off (Coupling Cap) | $f_{L,C_C} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi (R_S + r_\pi)\, C_C}$ | Hz | Dominant pole for $C_C$ | | 8 | Lower Cut-off (Bypass Cap) | $f_{L,C_E} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi (r_e' \parallel R_E)\, C_E}$ | Hz | Usually dominant pole | | 9 | Upper Cut-off (Miller) | $f_H = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_\text{eq} C_\text{in,total}}$ | Hz | $C_{in,\text{total}} = C_{be} + C_{bc}(1+\vert A_v \vert)$ | | 10 | Bandwidth | $BW = f_H - f_L \approx f_H$ | Hz | For $f_H \gg f_L$ | | 11 | Input Resistance (BJT) | $R_{in} = R_1 \parallel R_2 \parallel r_\pi$ | $\Omega$ | — | | 12 | Output Resistance (BJT) | $R_{out} \approx R_C \parallel r_o$ | $\Omega$ | $r_o \to \infty \Rightarrow R_{out} \approx R_C$ | | 13 | Transconductance | $g_m = \dfrac{I_C}{V_T} = \dfrac{1}{r_e'}$ | S (A/V) | — | | 14 | Power Gain | $A_P = A_v^2 \cdot \dfrac{R_{in}}{R_L}$ | dimensionless | — | ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this experiment matters in industry:** > - **Audio Preamplifiers:** The phono pre-amp in every record player and the microphone pre-amp in a mixing console are two or three RC coupled CE stages. > - **Hearing Aids:** Multi-stage low-voltage RC coupled amplifiers using micropower transistors. > - **Oscilloscope Input Stages:** Vertical input amplifiers are wide-band RC coupled chains. > - **Communication Receivers:** The Intermediate Frequency (IF) amplifier at 455 kHz in AM and 10.7 MHz in FM is essentially an RC coupled amplifier with tuned loads. > - **Biomedical Instrumentation:** ECG/EEG signal conditioning front-ends. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Mid-Band Voltage Gain of a CE Stage We begin with the small-signal hybrid-π model of a BJT, where $r_\pi$ is the resistance between base and emitter and a dependent current source $g_m \cdot v_{be}$ flows from collector to emitter. **Step 1 — Replace the input coupling capacitor by a short circuit (mid-band assumption).** The input AC voltage $v_{in}$ appears directly across the base-emitter junction: $$v_{be} = v_{in}$$ **Step 2 — Express the small-signal base current.** Applying Ohm's law across $r_\pi$: $$i_b = \frac{v_{be}}{r_\pi} = \frac{v_{in}}{r_\pi}$$ **Step 3 — Express the small-signal collector current.** Using the current gain relationship $i_c = \beta \cdot i_b$: $$i_c = \beta \cdot i_b = \frac{\beta \cdot v_{in}}{r_\pi}$$ **Step 4 — Identify the effective AC load at the collector.** With the emitter bypass capacitor shorting $R_E$ for AC and assuming the output coupling capacitor passes the load $R_L$ to the collector node: $$R_{AC} = R_C \parallel R_L$$ **Step 5 — Compute the output voltage.** The output voltage equals the voltage drop across $R_{AC}$ due to the collector current (KVL at the collector node): $$v_{out} = -i_c \cdot R_{AC} = -\frac{\beta \cdot v_{in} \cdot R_{AC}}{r_\pi}$$ **Step 6 — Form the voltage gain ratio.** $$A_v = \frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} = -\frac{\beta \cdot R_{AC}}{r_\pi}$$ **Step 7 — Simplify using $r_\pi = \beta \cdot r_e'$.** $$A_v = -\frac{\beta \cdot R_{AC}}{\beta \cdot r_e'} = -\frac{R_{AC}}{r_e'}$$ Therefore the **mid-band voltage gain** is: $$\boxed{A_v = -\frac{R_C \parallel R_L}{r_e'}}$$ ### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Lower Cut-off Frequency Due to the Coupling Capacitor The coupling capacitor $C_C$ introduces a high-pass filter behavior with the Thevenin resistance seen at its terminals. **Step 1 — Find the Thevenin resistance seen by $C_C$.** To the left of $C_C$: the source resistance $R_S$ (plus any series resistance). To the right of $C_C$: the input resistance of the next stage, $R_{in} = R_1 \parallel R_2 \parallel r_\pi$. The total series resistance is: $$R_{th} = R_S + (R_1 \parallel R_2 \parallel r_\pi)$$ **Step 2 — Write the transfer function of the high-pass RC network.** $$\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{R_{in}}{R_{th} + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_C}} \cdot \frac{1}{\text{(attenuation factor)}}$$ In standard form, the high-pass transfer function is: $$H(j\omega) = \frac{j\omega R_{th} C_C}{1 + j\omega R_{th} C_C}$$ **Step 3 — Identify the cutoff frequency as the frequency at which $\vert H \vert = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$.** This occurs when $\omega R_{th} C_C = 1$, giving: $$\boxed{f_L = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{th} C_C} = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_S + R_{in}) C_C}}$$ ### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Lower Cut-off Frequency Due to the Emitter Bypass Capacitor The bypass capacitor $C_E$ and the emitter resistor $R_E$ form a high-pass network at the emitter. **Step 1 — The resistance seen by $C_E$ is $R_E \parallel r_e'$.** When looking from the $C_E$ terminals, the emitter resistor $R_E$ is in parallel with the dynamic emitter resistance $r_e'$ (transformed via the BJT). $$R_{E,\text{eq}} = R_E \parallel r_e'$$ **Step 2 — Apply the standard high-pass formula.** $$\boxed{f_{L,E} = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_E \parallel r_e')\, C_E}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > Since $r_e' \ll R_E$, we have $R_E \parallel r_e' \approx r_e'$, making this pole **dominant** (higher $f_L$) compared to the coupling capacitor pole. Hence, the overall lower cutoff of a CE stage is set by $C_E$. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Removing Each Component | Component Removed | Q-Point | $A_v$ (Mid-band) | $f_L$ | $f_H$ | Stability | |-------------------|---------|------------------|-------|-------|-----------| | $C_C$ (input) | **Drifts** | Drops to zero (DC blocks signal) | — | — | Unstable | | $C_E$ | Stable | Drops to $-\dfrac{R_C}{R_E}$ (~5–10) | Increases | Same | Unchanged | | $R_E$ | **Unstable** | High | Same | Same | Thermal runaway risk | | $R_C$ | — | ~1 (no voltage gain) | — | — | — | | $R_1$ or $R_2$ | **Wrong bias** | 0 or distorted | — | — | Cut-off or saturation | | $C_{C2}$ (inter-stage) | Stage 2 biased wrong | 0 | — | — | Severe | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Two-Stage RC Coupled Amplifier — Circuit Schematic > [!NOTE] > The following Mermaid block renders a **functional block diagram** of the two-stage amplifier showing the role of each component. The schematic is adapted to the Mermaid engine for guaranteed compilation. ```mermaid graph LR sourceA["Vin AC Source"] --> cca["C_C1
Coupling Cap"] cca --> rdivA["R1 R2
Voltage Divider"] rdivA --> bjtBaseA["BJT Q1 Base"] bjtBaseA --> bjtEmitterA["BJT Q1 Emitter"] bjtEmitterA --> reA["R_E1"] reA --> ceA["C_E1
Bypass Cap"] ceA --> groundA["GND"] bjtBaseA --> bjtCollectorA["BJT Q1 Collector"] bjtCollectorA --> rcA["R_C1
Collector Load"] rcA --> vccA["V_CC Supply"] bjtCollectorA --> cc2A["C_C2
Inter-stage Coupling"] cc2A --> rLoadA["R Load of Stage 2"] rLoadA --> stageTwoA["STAGE 2 CE Amplifier"] stageTwoA --> voutA["Vout Final"] ``` ### 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Role of Each Component ```mermaid graph TD inputA["Input Signal v_in"] --> blockA["C_C1 BLOCKS DC
COUPLES AC"] blockA --> blockB["R1 R2 SET Q-POINT
VOLTAGE DIVIDER"] blockB --> blockC["Q1 BJT AMPLIFIES
ACTIVE DEVICE"] blockC --> blockD["R_C CONVERTS I TO V
OUTPUT LOAD"] blockD --> blockE["C_E BYPASSES R_E
FOR AC SIGNALS"] blockE --> blockF["C_C2 INTER-STAGE
DC BLOCK AND AC COUPLER"] blockF --> blockG["STAGE 2 BJT Q2
FURTHER AMPLIFIES"] blockG --> outputA["Output Signal v_out"] reBlock["R_E PROVIDES DC
THERMAL STABILITY"] --> blockE vccBlock["V_CC DC SUPPLY
FILTERED BY C_BYPASS"] --> blockC ``` ### 4.3 Frequency Response Curve (Conceptual) ```mermaid graph LR axis["Frequency f (log scale)"] --> lowF["Low Frequency f_L"] axis --> midF["Mid-band Flat Region"] axis --> highF["High Frequency f_H"] lowF --> lowG["Gain falls at
-20 dB/decade"] midF --> midG["Constant Gain
A_v max"] highF --> highG["Gain falls at
-20 dB/decade
per stage"] lowG --> midG midG --> highG ``` ### 4.4 Signal Flow from Input to Output (Detailed) ```mermaid graph TD startA["Input v_in"] --> capIn["C_C1 Input Coupling"] capIn --> bias1["R1 R2 Bias Network"] bias1 --> q1base["Base of Q1"] q1base --> q1emitter["Emitter of Q1"] q1emitter --> re1["R_E1 Emitter Resistor"] re1 --> ce1["C_E1 Bypass Capacitor"] ce1 --> gnd1["Ground"] q1base --> q1collector["Collector of Q1"] q1collector --> rc1["R_C1 Collector Resistor"] rc1 --> vcc1["V_CC"] q1collector --> capInt["C_C2 Inter-stage Coupling"] capInt --> bias2["R1 R2 Bias of Stage 2"] bias2 --> q2base["Base of Q2"] q2base --> q2emitter["Emitter of Q2"] q2emitter --> re2["R_E2"] re2 --> ce2["C_E2"] ce2 --> gnd2["Ground"] q2base --> q2collector["Collector of Q2"] q2collector --> rc2["R_C2"] rc2 --> vcc2["V_CC"] q2collector --> outputSig["Output v_out to Load"] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *What is the function of the emitter bypass capacitor $C_E$ in an RC coupled amplifier? What happens to the voltage gain if $C_E$ is removed?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The emitter bypass capacitor $C_E$ provides a low-reactance path to ground for AC signals, effectively short-circuiting the emitter resistor $R_E$ for AC. This restores the high voltage gain of the CE stage. - **With $C_E$ (mid-band):** $A_v = -\dfrac{R_C \parallel R_L}{r_e'}$ (gain $\approx$ 50–200) - **Without $C_E$:** $A_v = -\dfrac{R_C \parallel R_L}{r_e' + R_E} \approx -\dfrac{R_C}{R_E}$ (gain drops to 5–10) - $C_E$ also sets the lower cutoff frequency $f_{L,E} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi (R_E \parallel r_e') C_E}$. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse $R_E$ with $r_e'$. $R_E$ is the **external** swamping resistor (kΩ range), while $r_e'$ is the **internal** dynamic emitter resistance (few ohms to tens of ohms). Mixing these up will give a wrong answer. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Why is the coupling capacitor $C_C$ essential in a multi-stage RC coupled amplifier? Can we replace it with a wire?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The coupling capacitor $C_C$ blocks the DC bias voltage of one stage from reaching the next stage while allowing the AC signal to pass through. In a multi-stage amplifier, each stage has its own DC operating point (Q-point) that must be independent of the others. - **If $C_C$ is replaced by a wire:** The DC collector voltage of stage 1 (e.g., 6 V) will be directly applied to the base of stage 2, completely destroying its bias. Stage 2 will saturate, and amplification will cease. - The capacitor $C_C$ offers $X_C = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f C_C} \to 0$ at signal frequencies (AC pass) and $X_C \to \infty$ at DC (DC block). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write "AC couples and DC blocks" but **do not explain the consequence** of removing the capacitor. Always state the *resulting damage* to the next stage's bias to score full marks. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > As per KTU 2024 regulations, Part B questions carry internal choice. Two fully independent alternatives are provided below. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — Design-Oriented** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]** > *Design a single-stage RC coupled CE amplifier using a silicon BJT (β = 100) with $V_{CC} = 12$ V to drive a load $R_L = 4.7$ kΩ. The desired mid-band voltage gain is $|A_v| = 50$. Assume $V_{BE} = 0.7$ V and the lower cutoff frequency due to the bypass capacitor should be below 100 Hz.* > > **(a)** [7 marks] Calculate suitable values of $R_C$, $R_E$, $C_E$ and the bias resistors $R_1$, $R_2$. > > **(b)** [7 marks] Draw the complete circuit diagram and explain the function of each component. Also, find the overall gain in dB. **Model Solution:** **(a) Design Calculations:** **Step 1 — Determine $r_e'$ from the gain requirement.** The mid-band voltage gain formula is: $$A_v = -\frac{R_C \parallel R_L}{r_e'}$$ Assuming the loading effect is small ($R_C \parallel R_L \approx 0.5 R_C$ to $0.7 R_C$), we choose a target $R_{AC} = R_C \parallel R_L = 50 \cdot r_e' \Rightarrow r_e' = \dfrac{R_{AC}}{50}$. Choose a standard $R_C = 4.7$ kΩ. Then: $$R_{AC} = R_C \parallel R_L = \frac{4.7 \text{k} \times 4.7 \text{k}}{4.7 \text{k} + 4.7 \text{k}} = 2.35\ \text{k}\Omega$$ $$\therefore\ r_e' = \frac{2.35\ \text{k}\Omega}{50} = 47\ \Omega$$ **Step 2 — Calculate the emitter current $I_E$.** $$r_e' = \frac{26\ \text{mV}}{I_E\ (\text{mA})} \Rightarrow I_E = \frac{26}{47} \approx 0.553\ \text{mA}$$ Choose **$I_E \approx 1$ mA** for headroom (round $r_e' = 26$ Ω). Then: $$A_v = -\frac{2350}{26} \approx -90$$ To match $|A_v| = 50$, we choose $R_C = 1.3$ kΩ. Recalculate: $$R_{AC} = 1.3\text{k} \parallel 4.7\text{k} = \frac{1.3 \times 4.7}{1.3 + 4.7}\ \text{k}\Omega = \frac{6.11}{6.0} \approx 1.02\ \text{k}\Omega$$ With $I_E = 1$ mA, $r_e' = 26\ \Omega$: $$A_v = -\frac{1020}{26} \approx -39.2$$ To achieve exactly 50, choose $I_E = 0.5$ mA, $r_e' = 52\ \Omega$, $R_C = 4.7$ kΩ: $$R_{AC} = 4.7\text{k} \parallel 4.7\text{k} = 2.35\ \text{k}\Omega,\ A_v = -\frac{2350}{52} \approx -45.2$$ This is close to 50. **Final design choice:** $I_E = 0.5$ mA, $r_e' = 52$ Ω, $R_C = 4.7$ kΩ, giving $|A_v| \approx 45$ (acceptable for design). **Step 3 — Choose $R_E$ for bias stability.** The DC voltage across $R_E$ should be about **10% of $V_{CC}$** to allow maximum undistorted output swing: $$V_{R_E} = 0.1 \times 12 = 1.2\ \text{V}$$ $$R_E = \frac{V_{R_E}}{I_E} = \frac{1.2\ \text{V}}{0.5\ \text{mA}} = 2.4\ \text{k}\Omega$$ Choose standard value **$R_E = 2.2$ kΩ**. **Step 4 — Calculate bias resistors $R_1$ and $R_2$.** The base voltage is: $$V_B = V_{BE} + V_{R_E} = 0.7 + 1.2 = 1.9\ \text{V}$$ For bias stability, the base current $I_B = I_C / \beta = 0.5\ \text{mA} / 100 = 5\ \mu$A. Choose the divider current $I_2 = 10 I_B = 50\ \mu$A for good stability. $$R_2 = \frac{V_B}{I_2} = \frac{1.9\ \text{V}}{50\ \mu\text{A}} = 38\ \text{k}\Omega$$ $$R_1 = \frac{V_{CC} - V_B}{I_1} \approx \frac{V_{CC} - V_B}{I_2 + I_B} = \frac{12 - 1.9}{55\ \mu\text{A}} = 183.6\ \text{k}\Omega$$ Choose standard values: **$R_1 = 180$ kΩ, $R_2 = 39$ kΩ**. **Step 5 — Choose $C_E$ to satisfy $f_{L,E} < 100$ Hz.** The lower cutoff due to $C_E$ is: $$f_{L,E} = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_E \parallel r_e')\, C_E}$$ $$R_E \parallel r_e' = 2200 \parallel 52 \approx \frac{2200 \times 52}{2252} \approx 50.8\ \Omega$$ To make $f_{L,E} = 50$ Hz (well below 100 Hz): $$C_E = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 50.8 \times 50} \approx 62.7\ \mu\text{F}$$ Choose standard value **$C_E = 100\ \mu\text{F}$** (electrolytic, polarized). **Step 6 — Choose coupling capacitors $C_{C1}$ and $C_{C2}$.** With source resistance $R_S = 600\ \Omega$ (signal generator), $R_{in} = R_1 \parallel R_2 \parallel r_\pi$: $$r_\pi = \beta \cdot r_e' = 100 \times 52 = 5200\ \Omega = 5.2\ \text{k}\Omega$$ $$R_1 \parallel R_2 = \frac{180 \times 39}{219} \approx 32.05\ \text{k}\Omega$$ $$R_{in} = 32.05 \text{k} \parallel 5.2 \text{k} \approx 4.47\ \text{k}\Omega$$ $$R_{th} = R_S + R_{in} = 0.6 \text{k} + 4.47 \text{k} = 5.07\ \text{k}\Omega$$ To make $f_{L,C} = 20$ Hz (well below 100 Hz): $$C_C = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 5070 \times 20} \approx 1.57\ \mu\text{F}$$ Choose **$C_C = 4.7\ \mu\text{F}$** (electrolytic, polarized). **(b) Circuit Diagram and Gain in dB:** **Final Component List:** | Component | Value | Type | |-----------|-------|------| | $R_1$ | 180 kΩ | 1/4 W resistor | | $R_2$ | 39 kΩ | 1/4 W resistor | | $R_C$ | 4.7 kΩ | 1/4 W resistor | | $R_E$ | 2.2 kΩ | 1/4 W resistor | | $C_{C1}, C_{C2}$ | 4.7 µF | Electrolytic | | $C_E$ | 100 µF | Electrolytic | | Q1 | BC547 (β = 100) | NPN BJT | | $V_{CC}$ | +12 V | DC supply | **Functions of Each Component (as given in Section 2.2 above).** **Gain in dB:** $$A_v(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10} \vert A_v \vert = 20 \log_{10}(45) \approx 33.06\ \text{dB}$$ For a **two-stage** amplifier: $A_{v,\text{total}} = 45 \times 45 = 2025$ $$A_{v,\text{total}}(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}(2025) \approx 66.13\ \text{dB}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > - **Do not** round $r_e'$ to 26 Ω if the calculated $I_E$ is different. Always re-evaluate $r_e' = 26\ \text{mV} / I_E(\text{mA})$. > - **Do not** forget to apply the **loading effect** ($R_C \parallel R_L$), not just $R_C$. > - **Do not** state $A_{v,\text{total}} = A_{v1} + A_{v2}$ — this is a common error; the correct relation is **product**, not sum. > - Always convert final gain to **dB** for full marks. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Analysis-Oriented** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** > *For a two-stage RC coupled amplifier using identical CE stages, the following data are measured:* > - *Mid-band voltage gain per stage: $|A_{v1}| = |A_{v2}| = 40$* > - *Lower cutoff frequency of stage 1: $f_{L1} = 80$ Hz* > - *Lower cutoff frequency of stage 2: $f_{L2} = 60$ Hz* > - *Upper cutoff frequency of stage 1: $f_{H1} = 250$ kHz* > - *Upper cutoff frequency of stage 2: $f_{H2} = 200$ kHz* > - *Input signal: $V_{in} = 10$ mV (sine wave, 1 kHz)* > > **(a)** [7 marks] Calculate the overall mid-band voltage gain, the overall gain in dB, and the output voltage at 1 kHz. > > **(b)** [7 marks] Find the overall lower and upper cutoff frequencies of the cascaded amplifier. Explain the phase relationship between input and output. **Model Solution:** **(a) Overall Gain and Output Voltage:** **Step 1 — Overall mid-band voltage gain (product of individual gains):** $$A_{v,\text{total}} = A_{v1} \cdot A_{v2} = (-40) \times (-40) = +1600$$ The **positive** sign indicates that the **two 180° phase shifts cancel**, so the final output is **in phase** with the input. **Step 2 — Convert to decibels:** $$A_{v,\text{total}}(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}(1600) = 20 \times 3.204 = 64.08\ \text{dB}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the output voltage at 1 kHz (mid-band):** $$V_{out} = A_{v,\text{total}} \times V_{in} = 1600 \times 10\ \text{mV} = 16{,}000\ \text{mV} = \mathbf{16\ V\ (peak)}$$ If RMS: $V_{out,\text{rms}} = 1600 \times \frac{10\ \text{mV}}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 11.31\ \text{V (rms)}$. **(b) Overall Cutoff Frequencies and Phase:** **Step 1 — Overall lower cutoff frequency.** For cascaded non-identical stages, the overall response is the product of individual responses. Solving for the −3 dB overall frequency: $$f_{L,\text{overall}} \approx \sqrt{f_{L1}^2 + f_{L2}^2 - 2 f_L^2} \approx 1.1 \sqrt{f_{L1}^2 + f_{L2}^2}$$ (approximately the **dominant-pole** or **sum of squared cutoffs** approach). Using the practical approximation: $$f_{L,\text{overall}} \approx 1.1 \sqrt{80^2 + 60^2} = 1.1 \sqrt{6400 + 3600} = 1.1 \sqrt{10000} = 1.1 \times 100 = \mathbf{110\ Hz}$$ A more exact approach using decibel addition: $f_{L,\text{overall}}$ is the frequency where the total attenuation from both stages equals −3 dB. Numerical solution yields approximately **106 Hz**. **Step 2 — Overall upper cutoff frequency.** Using the same squared-sum approximation: $$f_{H,\text{overall}} \approx \frac{1}{\sqrt{\frac{1}{f_{H1}^2} + \frac{1}{f_{H2}^2}}}$$ $$= \frac{1}{\sqrt{\frac{1}{250000^2} + \frac{1}{200000^2}}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1.6 \times 10^{-11} + 2.5 \times 10^{-11}}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{4.1 \times 10^{-11}}}$$ $$= \frac{1}{6.4 \times 10^{-6}} \approx \mathbf{156\ kHz}$$ **Step 3 — Phase relationship.** Each CE stage introduces a **180° phase reversal** (gain is negative). For two cascaded stages: $$\phi_{\text{total}} = 180° + 180° = 360° \equiv 0°$$ Therefore, the final output is **in phase** with the input. However, at the cutoff frequencies, each stage adds an additional **45° phase shift** (the low-frequency pole contributes +45° to +90° near $f_L$, and the high-frequency pole contributes −45° to −90° near $f_H$). At $f_{L,\text{overall}}$ and $f_{H,\text{overall}}$, the phase shift is approximately ±90° from the mid-band value. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > - **Common Mistake 1:** Writing $A_{v,\text{total}} = A_{v1} + A_{v2} = 80$ — **wrong**. It is the **product** ($40 \times 40 = 1600$). This single error will cost 3–4 marks. > - **Common Mistake 2:** Stating the output is **180° out of phase** with the input for a two-stage amplifier. It is **in phase (0°)** because two inversions cancel. > - **Common Mistake 3:** Using the simple formula $f_{L,\text{overall}} = f_{L1} + f_{L2}$. This is **not** correct; the proper treatment is via squared sums or solving the product of transfer functions. ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist (must memorize before exam):** - **RC coupled amplifier** = multi-stage CE amplifier with capacitor coupling — most common AF amplifier. - **Each stage provides 180° phase shift**; two stages give 360° ≡ 0° (in-phase output). - **Mid-band gain of one CE stage:** $A_v = -R_C \parallel R_L \,/\, r_e'$ (negative sign mandatory). - **$r_e' = 26\ \text{mV} / I_E(\text{mA})$** — must be computed for the *actual* bias current, not assumed as 26 Ω. - **Coupling capacitor $C_C$**: blocks DC, passes AC. Sets $f_{L,C_C} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi (R_S + R_{in}) C_C}$. - **Bypass capacitor $C_E$**: shorts $R_E$ for AC, restoring high gain. Sets $f_{L,E} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi (R_E \parallel r_e') C_E}$ — **dominant** lower pole. - **Emitter resistor $R_E$**: provides DC thermal stability; must be bypassed by $C_E$ to avoid gain loss. - **Collector resistor $R_C$**: converts $i_c$ variations into $v_{out}$; without it, no voltage gain. - **Bias resistors $R_1$, $R_2$**: voltage divider sets $V_B \approx V_{BE} + I_E R_E$. - **Overall gain (two stages):** $A_{v,\text{total}} = A_{v1} \cdot A_{v2}$ — **product, never sum**. - **Gain in dB:** $A_v(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10} \vert A_v \vert$. - **Frequency response:** Flat mid-band, falls at −20 dB/decade at low and high frequencies. - **Miller effect:** $C_{bc}$ is multiplied by $(1 + A_v)$ at the input, reducing $f_H$. - **Why capacitors fail to work:** If $C_C$ or $C_E$ is **open** (fault), the lower cutoff frequency shoots up — a common lab test question. - **Practical lab procedure:** Always measure $V_{CEQ}$ and $I_{CQ}$ first, then $r_e'$, then calculate expected gain and compare with measured value. - **KTU viva favorite:** *"What happens if you increase $R_C$?"* — Gain increases, but $V_{CEQ}$ drops; if $R_C$ is too large, the transistor saturates and clipping occurs. - **Output voltage swing limit:** $\pm (V_{CEQ} - V_{CE,\text{sat}}) \approx \pm 5$ V for $V_{CC} = 12$ V and $V_{CEQ} = 6$ V. > [!WARNING] > **Final Examiner's Reminder:** In lab exams, KTU evaluators specifically look for: (1) **Q-point calculation** before drawing the circuit, (2) **loading effect** in gain derivation, (3) **frequency response sketch with both cutoffs marked**, and (4) **dB conversion**. Skipping any of these will cost you 2–3 marks per item.

topics.title: Multistage Amplifiers - Effects of Cascading on Gain and Bandwidth
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPL307 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Multistage Amplifiers - Effects of Cascading on Gain and Bandwidth

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

A **Multistage Amplifier** is a cascaded arrangement of two or more single-stage amplifier blocks coupled together through a coupling network (RC, transformer, or direct) such that the output of one stage drives the input of the next, with the primary objectives of achieving **higher overall voltage gain**, **broader bandwidth**, **improved input/output impedance characteristics**, and **enhanced signal fidelity** than any single stage could provide in isolation.

In the context of **PCCPL307 – Analog and Digital Electronics Lab (KTU 2024 Scheme)**, Module Experiment 2 specifically investigates how cascading two identical CE (Common Emitter) RC-coupled BJT amplifier stages alters two critical figure-of-merit parameters:

- **Mid-band Voltage Gain** $\rightarrow$ increases (multiplicatively)
- **Bandwidth (BW)** $\rightarrow$ shrinks (because each pole contributes to the overall roll-off)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:** "A cascade connection of amplifier stages is one where the output of one stage forms the input to the next stage, the overall gain being the product of individual stage gains expressed in linear scale, or sum of gains when expressed in decibels (dB)."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Think of a multistage amplifier like a **relay race team passing a baton**:

- **Stage 1 (Runner 1)** takes a weak, faint signal (the baton) and amplifies it — but it is still not strong enough to drive the loudspeaker directly.
- **Coupling Network (Handoff Zone)** is the RC/transformer link that passes the AC signal from one stage to the next while blocking the DC bias from contaminating the next stage's operating point.
- **Stage 2 (Runner 2)** receives this *already-amplified* signal and amplifies it further.
- **Final Output (Finish Line)** is now strong enough to drive a load (speaker, antenna, ADC input, etc.).

### Why does bandwidth SHRINK when we cascade?

Imagine each amplifier stage as a **low-pass filter** with its own internal capacitances (Miller capacitance, stray capacitance, coupling/bypass capacitors). When you cascade two filters, you get **two poles** on the frequency response, and the response falls off **faster** near the upper cutoff. The signal therefore cannot pass through as wide a range of frequencies → **bandwidth shrinks**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trade-off Rule of Thumb:** Cascading always *trades bandwidth for gain*. The **Gain–Bandwidth Product (GBP)** of the *system* is approximately conserved, but the *useful* bandwidth shrinks while the *mid-band* gain rises dramatically.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Standard Symbol | Typical Magnitude (CE BJT) |
|---|---|---|
| Mid-band voltage gain (single CE stage) | $A_v$ | **100 – 500** (linear), **40 – 54 dB** |
| Upper cutoff frequency (single stage) | $f_H$ | **100 kHz – 5 MHz** |
| Lower cutoff frequency (single stage) | $f_L$ | **20 Hz – 200 Hz** |
| Gain–Bandwidth Product | $GBP$ | **constant** for a given transistor |
| 3 dB bandwidth | $BW = f_H - f_L$ | dominated by $f_H$ since $f_L \ll f_H$ |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Cascade? — The Engineering Motivation

A single CE stage gives a gain of typically **40–50 dB**. Many real applications (audio power amplifiers, RF front-ends, instrumentation) demand **60–100 dB** total gain. Since transistor internal parameters (gm, $r_\pi$, $r_o$) cannot be arbitrarily manipulated without violating bias stability, the practical engineering solution is to **cascade two or more stable stages** rather than attempt to build one "super-stage."

## 2.2 Types of Coupling (KTU Classification)

| Coupling Type | Coupling Element | Frequency Response | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **RC Coupling** | Capacitor + Resistor divider | Wideband, good audio response | Audio amplifiers (most common in lab) |
| **Transformer Coupling** | Transformer | Resonant, narrow band, high gain | RF / tuned amplifiers |
| **Direct Coupling** | Wire (no element) | DC to high frequency, suffers drift | Op-amps, DC amplifiers, ICs |

> [!NOTE]
> In **PCCPL307 Module 2**, the experiment uses **RC-coupled CE–CE cascade** because the frequency response is easy to measure and analyse with the lab's function generator and CRO/DSO.

## 2.3 The Multiplicative Gain Rule

For $n$ stages cascaded in linear operation (no loading effects neglected), the **overall voltage gain** is:

$$A_{v(\text{total})} = A_{v1} \times A_{v2} \times A_{v3} \times \dots \times A_{vn}$$

Expressed in **decibels** (since the KTU 2024 scheme emphasises dB for measurement reporting):

$$A_{v(\text{total, dB})} = A_{v1(\text{dB})} + A_{v2(\text{dB})} + \dots + A_{vn(\text{dB})}$$

This is why gains in dB **add** — because logarithms convert multiplication into addition.

## 2.4 Effect of Cascading on Bandwidth — The Pole-Multiplication Problem

Each amplifier stage contributes a **dominant pole** at $f_{H1}, f_{H2}, \dots, f_{Hn}$. The overall transfer function becomes:

$$A_v(s) = \frac{A_{v(\text{mid})}}{\left(1 + \dfrac{s}{2\pi f_{H1}}\right)\left(1 + \dfrac{s}{2\pi f_{H2}}\right)\dots\left(1 + \dfrac{s}{2\pi f_{Hn}}\right)}$$

The magnitude falls at a rate of **$-20n$ dB/decade** beyond the dominant pole, which is much steeper than a single stage's **$-20$ dB/decade**. This is why **the effective upper cutoff frequency $f_H$ decreases** as we cascade.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **minimum required formula set** for the Module 2 lab viva and ESE. Master every cell.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A_{v,t} = \prod_{k=1}^{n} A_{vk}$ | Overall linear gain | unitless (V/V) |
| 2 | $A_{v,t(dB)} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} A_{vk(dB)}$ | Overall dB gain | dB |
| 3 | $A_{vk(dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(\vert A_{vk}\vert)$ | Convert linear to dB | dB |
| 4 | $BW = f_H - f_L \approx f_H$ | 3 dB bandwidth (audio) | Hz |
| 5 | $f_{H(\text{cascaded}, n)} = f_{H1}\sqrt{2^{1/n} - 1}$ | Identical stages bandwidth shrinkage | Hz |
| 6 | $f_{H(\text{cascaded})} \approx \dfrac{0.83}{n}\,f_{H1}$ (approx. for $n\ge2$) | Quick estimate | Hz |
| 7 | $GBP = \vert A_{v(\text{mid})}\vert \times f_H$ | Gain–Bandwidth Product | Hz |
| 8 | $f_{H(\text{non-identical})} \approx \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{f_{H1}^{2}} + \dfrac{1}{f_{H2}^{2}}}}$ | Two dissimilar stages | Hz |
| 9 | $f_{L(\text{cascaded})} \approx \sqrt{f_{L1}^{2} + f_{L2}^{2}}$ | Lower cutoff (for $f_L \ll f_H$) | Hz |
| 10 | $t_r = \dfrac{0.35}{f_H}$ | Rise time (10%–90%) — single stage | s |
| 11 | $t_{r,\text{total}} = 1.1\sqrt{t_{r1}^{2} + t_{r2}^{2}}$ | Rise time of cascade | s |
| 12 | Roll-off slope | $-20$ dB/dec (single), $-40$ dB/dec (two-stage) | dB/decade |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU exams:** "*Gain MULTIPLIES in linear scale, ADDS in dB, while BANDWIDTH SHRINKS by the $\sqrt{2^{1/n}-1}$ factor for identical stages.*"

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Audio Power Amplifiers** (e.g., 100 W stereo): 3-stage cascade — pre-amp (high $Z_{in}$), driver (voltage gain), power stage (current gain).
- **Oscilloscope Front-End**: cascaded wideband amplifiers to drive the CRT/ADC with minimal distortion.
- **RF Receivers**: tuned RF amplifier + IF amplifier + audio amplifier (superheterodyne architecture).
- **Instrumentation (ECG, EEG machines)**: instrumentation cascade provides > 80 dB common-mode rejection AND high differential gain.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Bandwidth Shrinkage Factor for $n$ Identical Cascaded Stages

**Starting point** — For a single low-pass stage with mid-band gain $A_0$ and upper cutoff $f_{H1}$:

$$\left\vert A_{v1}(f) \right\vert = \frac{A_0}{\sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{f}{f_{H1}}\right)^{2}}}$$

For $n$ *identical* such stages cascaded:

$$\left\vert A_{vn}(f) \right\vert = \frac{A_0^{\,n}}{\left[\sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{f}{f_{H1}}\right)^{2}}\right]^{n}} = \frac{(A_0)^{n}}{\left[1 + \left(\dfrac{f}{f_{H1}}\right)^{2}\right]^{n/2}}$$

**Definition of new upper cutoff** $f_{Hn}$: it is the frequency at which the overall gain falls to $1/\sqrt{2}$ of its mid-band value:

$$\frac{(A_0)^{n}}{\left[1 + \left(\dfrac{f_{Hn}}{f_{H1}}\right)^{2}\right]^{n/2}} = \frac{(A_0)^{n}}{\sqrt{2}}$$

Taking $(2)^{1/2}$ power on both sides:

$$\left[1 + \left(\dfrac{f_{Hn}}{f_{H1}}\right)^{2}\right]^{n/2} = 2^{1/2}$$

Raising both sides to power $2/n$:

$$1 + \left(\dfrac{f_{Hn}}{f_{H1}}\right)^{2} = 2^{1/n}$$

Rearranging:

$$\left(\dfrac{f_{Hn}}{f_{H1}}\right)^{2} = 2^{1/n} - 1$$

Taking square root:

$$\boxed{\,f_{Hn} = f_{H1}\,\sqrt{\,2^{1/n} - 1\,}\,}$$

**Numerical Verification Table** (KTU often asks "what is the bandwidth for 2 stages?"):

| $n$ | $2^{1/n}$ | $\sqrt{2^{1/n}-1}$ | $f_{Hn}/f_{H1}$ | Bandwidth Shrinkage |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2.0000 | 1.0000 | **1.0000** | Reference (no shrinkage) |
| 2 | 1.4142 | 0.6436 | **0.644** | ~36% reduction |
| 3 | 1.2599 | 0.5098 | **0.510** | ~49% reduction |
| 4 | 1.1892 | 0.4350 | **0.435** | ~57% reduction |
| 5 | 1.1487 | 0.3856 | **0.386** | ~61% reduction |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key insight for KTU 2024 exams:** Doubling the number of stages cuts the bandwidth to **64.4%** of the single-stage value, but quadruples the dB gain. This is the heart of the *Gain–Bandwidth Trade-off*.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Gain–Bandwidth Product (GBP) Invariance

For a CE stage using a single dominant-pole approximation:

$$A_v(s) = \frac{A_{v(\text{mid})}}{1 + \dfrac{s}{\omega_H}} \quad \text{where} \quad \omega_H = 2\pi f_H$$

The **Gain–Bandwidth Product** is defined as:

$$GBP = \vert A_{v(\text{mid})} \vert \times f_H$$

For a single BJT CE stage in the mid-band, $A_{v(\text{mid})} = -g_m R_C$ and $f_H = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_\text{eq} C_\text{eq}}$, where $C_\text{eq}$ includes the Miller-multiplied $C_{bc}$.

The GBP becomes:

$$GBP = \left(\frac{g_m R_C}{2\pi R_\text{eq} C_\text{eq}}\right)$$

For a *single transistor*, the GBP is **fixed by device physics** (largely by $g_m$ and the Miller capacitance $C_{bc}$). Therefore:

$$\boxed{\,A_{v(\text{mid})} \times f_H \approx \text{constant for a given transistor}\,}$$

**However** — in a cascade, the *system GBP* is **NOT** simply $n \times (\text{single-stage GBP})$. Because the poles interact, the *system* GBP actually improves, but not linearly. For two identical stages:

$$GBP_\text{system} = A_{v,\text{total}}^{2} \times f_{H2} = (A_0^2)(0.644\,f_{H1}) = 0.644\,(A_0^2 f_{H1})$$

Whereas one stage: $GBP_1 = A_0 \cdot f_{H1}$. The ratio:

$$\frac{GPB_\text{2-stage}}{GBP_\text{1-stage}} = 0.644\,A_0$$

For $A_0 = 100$ → ratio $= 64.4$. So cascading provides a *substantial* increase in *useful* system GBP, even though the bandwidth itself shrinks.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Lower Cutoff Frequency of a Cascade

For the lower cutoff, each stage has a high-pass characteristic with pole $f_{L1}$:

$$\left\vert A_{v}(f) \right\vert = \frac{A_0 \left(\dfrac{f}{f_{L1}}\right)}{\sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{f}{f_{L1}}\right)^{2}}}$$

For $n$ identical high-pass sections:

$$\left[1 + \left(\dfrac{f_{L1}}{f_{Ln}}\right)^{2}\right]^{n/2} = 2^{1/2}$$

By identical algebra to Derivation 1:

$$\boxed{\,f_{Ln} = \dfrac{f_{L1}}{\sqrt{2^{1/n} - 1}}\,}$$

**Note carefully:** For the *lower* cutoff, the cascade **raises** $f_L$ (worsens low-frequency response). For 2 stages:

$$f_{L2} = \frac{f_{L1}}{0.644} \approx 1.55\,f_{L1}$$

So low-frequency cutoff worsens by a factor of ~1.55 — **the bandwidth narrows from BOTH ends**.

## 3.4 Combined Bandwidth of a 2-Stage RC-Coupled CE Cascade

$$\text{Total } BW = f_{H2} - f_{L2} = 0.644\,f_{H1} - 1.55\,f_{L1}$$

Since $f_{L1} \ll f_{H1}$ (typically $f_L \sim 100$ Hz, $f_H \sim 100$ kHz):

$$BW_\text{cascade} \approx 0.644\,f_{H1} \quad \text{(3-dB bandwidth, approximated)}$$

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** Two identical CE stages each have a mid-band gain of **200** (linear) and an upper cutoff of **200 kHz**. Find (a) total gain in dB, (b) new upper cutoff, (c) GBP of single stage vs system, (d) bandwidth shrinkage %.

**Solution:**

**(a) Total gain in dB:**

$$A_{v1(dB)} = 20\log_{10}(200) = 20 \times 2.301 = 46.02 \text{ dB}$$

$$A_{v,\text{total}(dB)} = 2 \times 46.02 = 92.04 \text{ dB}$$

$$A_{v,\text{total(linear)}} = 200 \times 200 = 40{,}000$$

**(b) New upper cutoff for $n=2$:**

$$f_{H2} = f_{H1}\sqrt{2^{1/2} - 1} = 200{,}000 \times 0.6436 = 128{,}720 \text{ Hz} \approx 128.7 \text{ kHz}$$

**(c) GBP comparison:**

$$GBP_{\text{single}} = 200 \times 200 \text{ kHz} = 40 \text{ MHz}$$

$$GBP_{\text{system}} = 40{,}000 \times 128.7 \text{ kHz} = 5{,}148 \text{ MHz} \approx 5.15 \text{ GHz}$$

**(d) Bandwidth shrinkage:**

$$\text{Shrinkage} = \frac{f_{H1} - f_{H2}}{f_{H1}} \times 100\% = \frac{200 - 128.7}{200}\times 100\% = 35.65\%$$

> [!TIP]
> **For the lab record:** Always tabulate the **measured** values of $A_{v1}$, $A_{v2}$, $f_{H1}$, $f_{H2}$, $f_{L1}$, $f_{L2}$ and compare with the theoretical $0.644$ shrinkage factor. A 5–10% deviation from theory is acceptable due to component tolerances.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of a 2-Stage RC-Coupled CE Cascade

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Vs[Vin AC Source] --> C1[Coupling Capacitor Cc1]
    C1 --> S1[Stage 1 CE Amplifier]
    S1 --> C2[Interstage Coupling Capacitor Cc2]
    C2 --> S2[Stage 2 CE Amplifier]
    S2 --> C3[Output Coupling Capacitor Cc3]
    C3 --> RL[Load Resistor RL]
    S1 -- bias --> B1[VB1 Bias Network]
    S2 -- bias --> B2[VB2 Bias Network]
```

> **Figure 4.1 — Block-level signal flow of a 2-stage RC-coupled CE amplifier cascade.** Each `C` block represents a coupling/bypass capacitor that blocks DC between stages while allowing the AC signal to pass.

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of Cascading Effects

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Single CE Stage] --> B{Stage Parameters}
    B --> C[Mid-band Gain Av1 in dB]
    B --> D[Upper Cutoff fH1 in Hz]
    B --> E[Lower Cutoff fL1 in Hz]
    B --> F[Bandwidth BW1 in Hz]
    C --> G[Add in dB]
    D --> H[Shrink via sqrt factor]
    E --> I[Worsen via 1 by sqrt factor]
    G --> J[Overall Av total dB]
    H --> K[Overall fH n]
    I --> L[Overall fL n]
    K --> M[Total Bandwidth BWn]
    L --> M
    J --> N[Gain Bandwidth Product GBP]
    M --> N
    N --> O[Engineering Trade off Verification]
```

> **Figure 4.2 — Mapping the cascading effects on gain and bandwidth parameters** in the form of a computational logic flow used in lab data analysis.

## 4.3 Frequency Response (Bode Magnitude) — Conceptual Plot

The frequency response of a 2-stage cascade exhibits three distinct regions:

```
Gain |dB|
   |
92 |- - - - - - - - - ___________________________  (mid-band flat)
   |               /   slope: -20 dB/dec
46 |- - - - ___/   _____________________________ (single stage ref)
   |        /    /
   |       /  / slope: -40 dB/dec
   |      //
   |     //
 0 |____//__________________________________________
   |    /|
   |   / | fL1    fL2                  fH2   fH1
   |  /  |                                        
   |_/__|__________________________________________ freq |Hz|
       20 Hz    100 Hz          128 kHz   200 kHz
```

**Key observations from the plot:**

- **Mid-band gain** of cascade = 2 × single stage in dB.
- **Upper roll-off** slope is **−40 dB/decade** (steeper than single stage's −20 dB/decade).
- **Lower roll-off** slope is **+40 dB/decade** (steeper rise from 0 Hz).
- **3-dB bandwidth** is *narrower* than the single stage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Limitation:** Mermaid cannot natively render x-y Bode plots. The schematic above is a labelled ASCII representation conveying the salient features. In your lab record, redraw this neatly on graph paper with measured data points overlaid.

## 4.4 Equivalent Small-Signal Model Block Diagram (Functional)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Input Source vs] --> R1[Rs]
    R1 --> Cc1[Coupling Cap]
    Cc1 --> N1[Node at Base of Q1]
    N1 --> B1[Bias Divider R1 R2]
    N1 --> Q1[Q1 CE Transistor]
    Q1 --> RC1[Collector Resistor RC1]
    Q1 --> RE1[Emitter Bypass Cap CE1]
    RC1 --> Cc2[Interstage Cap]
    Cc2 --> N2[Node at Base of Q2]
    N2 --> B2[Bias Divider R3 R4]
    N2 --> Q2[Q2 CE Transistor]
    Q2 --> RC2[Collector Resistor RC2]
    Q2 --> RE2[Emitter Bypass Cap CE2]
    RC2 --> Cc3[Output Cap]
    Cc3 --> Vout[Vout]
```

> **Figure 4.3 — Functional block-level architecture** showing how bias networks, coupling capacitors, and emitter-bypass capacitors interconnect in a practical 2-stage CE cascade realised on a breadboard.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]`
**Define a multistage amplifier. Why are amplifier stages cascaded in practice, even though a single transistor can theoretically be biased for any gain?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) **Definition:** A multistage amplifier is a cascade connection of two or more amplifier stages such that the output of one drives the input of the next, with the overall gain being the product (linear) or sum (dB) of individual gains.
- (1 mark) **Reason 1 — Gain limitation:** A single BJT biased in CE configuration gives a stable mid-band gain of 40–60 dB; further gain increase via bias manipulation destabilises the Q-point.
- (1 mark) **Reason 2 — Impedance matching:** Input impedance of cascade can be made very high (small base current), output impedance very low (emitter follower as final stage), optimising power transfer.
- *(Bonus mention — Bandwidth vs Gain trade-off is also a valid answer.)*

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]`
**What is the Gain–Bandwidth Product (GBP)? State its significance in the context of cascaded amplifiers.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) **Definition:** GBP is the product of the mid-band gain $|A_{v(mid)}|$ and the upper 3-dB cutoff frequency $f_H$ of an amplifier: $GBP = |A_{v(mid)}| \times f_H$.
- (1 mark) **For a single transistor:** GBP is a constant determined by device physics ($g_m$ and Miller capacitance $C_{bc}$).
- (1 mark) **For a cascade:** Although the *system* bandwidth shrinks and the system gain rises as $A_0^n$, the *overall* GBP improves approximately as $0.644 \cdot A_0^{n-1}$ times the single-stage GBP (for $n$ identical stages). Hence cascading *does* enhance usable performance in wideband applications.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and offers internal choice. Each sub-part is 7 marks. Provide both Question A and Question B (alternatives) with full worked solutions.

---

### ✅ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**A two-stage RC-coupled CE amplifier uses identical transistors. Each stage has a mid-band gain of 200 and an upper cutoff frequency of 250 kHz.**

**(a)** Calculate the **overall mid-band voltage gain** in dB and the **new upper cutoff frequency** of the cascade. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the lower cutoff of each stage is **80 Hz**, find the **new lower cutoff** and the **overall 3-dB bandwidth** of the cascade. Also, comment on the **gain–bandwidth trade-off**. **(7 marks)**

#### 📘 Model Solution A

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

Step 1 — Gain in dB of one stage:
$$A_{v1(dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(200) = 20 \times 2.301 = 46.02 \text{ dB}$$

Step 2 — Overall gain in dB (cascaded):
$$A_{v,\text{total}(dB)} = 2 \times 46.02 = 92.04 \text{ dB}$$

Step 3 — Equivalent linear gain:
$$A_{v,\text{total(linear)}} = 200 \times 200 = 40{,}000$$

Step 4 — Upper cutoff for 2 identical stages:
$$f_{H2} = f_{H1}\sqrt{2^{1/2} - 1} = 250 \text{ kHz} \times 0.6436 = 160.9 \text{ kHz}$$

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

Step 5 — New lower cutoff for 2 identical stages:
$$f_{L2} = \frac{f_{L1}}{\sqrt{2^{1/2} - 1}} = \frac{80}{0.6436} = 124.3 \text{ Hz}$$

Step 6 — Total 3-dB bandwidth:
$$BW_\text{total} = f_{H2} - f_{L2} = 160{,}900 - 124.3 \approx 160.78 \text{ kHz}$$

Step 7 — Single-stage bandwidth:
$$BW_\text{single} = 250{,}000 - 80 = 249.92 \text{ kHz}$$

Step 8 — Bandwidth shrinkage:
$$\% \text{ shrinkage} = \frac{249.92 - 160.78}{249.92} \times 100 = 35.66\%$$

Step 9 — Gain–Bandwidth Product:
$$GBP_\text{single} = 200 \times 250 \text{ kHz} = 50 \text{ MHz}$$
$$GBP_\text{system} = 40{,}000 \times 160.9 \text{ kHz} = 6{,}436 \text{ MHz} \approx 6.44 \text{ GHz}$$

**Comment on Trade-off (1 mark embedded):**
The mid-band gain increased by **46 dB** (a factor of 200) while the bandwidth decreased by ~**36%**. The *system* GBP, however, increased by a factor of **128×** compared to the single-stage GBP. This demonstrates the classic engineering trade-off: **cascading trades bandwidth for gain, but the product improves dramatically**, justifying multistage design in high-gain applications.

---

### ✅ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]`

**A three-stage RC-coupled amplifier has individual stage mid-band gains of $A_1 = 50$, $A_2 = 80$, $A_3 = 40$ and upper cutoff frequencies of $f_{H1} = 300$ kHz, $f_{H2} = 400$ kHz, $f_{H3} = 500$ kHz respectively.**

**(a)** Compute the **total mid-band gain in dB** and the **effective upper cutoff frequency** of the cascade. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the **lower cutoff frequencies** are $f_{L1} = 100$ Hz, $f_{L2} = 150$ Hz, $f_{L3} = 200$ Hz, find the **overall lower cutoff** using the RMS approximation, and determine the **3-dB bandwidth** of the system. Also explain why the roll-off slope beyond $f_H$ is **−60 dB/decade**. **(7 marks)**

#### 📘 Model Solution B

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

Step 1 — Convert each gain to dB:
$$A_{1(dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(50) = 33.98 \text{ dB}$$
$$A_{2(dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(80) = 38.06 \text{ dB}$$
$$A_{3(dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(40) = 32.04 \text{ dB}$$

Step 2 — Total gain in dB:
$$A_{v,\text{total}(dB)} = 33.98 + 38.06 + 32.04 = 104.08 \text{ dB}$$

Step 3 — Total linear gain:
$$A_{v,\text{total}} = 50 \times 80 \times 40 = 160{,}000$$

Step 4 — Effective upper cutoff (RMS formula for non-identical stages):
$$\frac{1}{f_{H,\text{eff}}^{2}} = \frac{1}{f_{H1}^{2}} + \frac{1}{f_{H2}^{2}} + \frac{1}{f_{H3}^{2}}$$
$$\frac{1}{f_{H,\text{eff}}^{2}} = \frac{1}{(300\text{k})^{2}} + \frac{1}{(400\text{k})^{2}} + \frac{1}{(500\text{k})^{2}}$$
$$\frac{1}{f_{H,\text{eff}}^{2}} = 1.111\times 10^{-11} + 6.25\times 10^{-12} + 4.0\times 10^{-12}$$
$$\frac{1}{f_{H,\text{eff}}^{2}} = 2.136 \times 10^{-11}$$
$$f_{H,\text{eff}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2.136 \times 10^{-11}}} = 216{,}346 \text{ Hz} \approx 216.3 \text{ kHz}$$

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

Step 5 — Overall lower cutoff (RMS approximation):
$$f_{L,\text{total}} = \sqrt{f_{L1}^{2} + f_{L2}^{2} + f_{L3}^{2}}$$
$$f_{L,\text{total}} = \sqrt{100^{2} + 150^{2} + 200^{2}} = \sqrt{10{,}000 + 22{,}500 + 40{,}000}$$
$$f_{L,\text{total}} = \sqrt{72{,}500} = 269.3 \text{ Hz}$$

Step 6 — 3-dB Bandwidth:
$$BW = f_{H,\text{eff}} - f_{L,\text{total}} = 216{,}346 - 269.3 \approx 216.08 \text{ kHz}$$

Step 7 — Explanation of −60 dB/decade slope:
Each of the three amplifier stages contributes a single dominant pole, giving a roll-off of −20 dB/decade per stage. When cascaded, the slopes add:
$$\text{Total roll-off} = -20 \times 3 = -60 \text{ dB/decade}$$

This steep roll-off is both an advantage (better high-frequency rejection of noise) and a disadvantage (faster gain loss above cutoff, narrower usable bandwidth).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert to dB** when stage gains are given in linear scale (loses 1–2 marks).
> 2. **Using $f_{H2} = 0.5 \times f_{H1}$** instead of $0.644 \times f_{H1}$ (wrong shrinkage factor). The correct formula is $f_{Hn} = f_{H1}\sqrt{2^{1/n}-1}$, NOT a simple halving.
> 3. **Mixing up upper and lower cutoff formulas** — they are reciprocals of each other! Lower cutoff *increases* (worsens) with cascading, upper cutoff *decreases*.
> 4. **Failing to mention the trade-off explicitly** when asked about cascading — the question is asking for the *engineering interpretation*, not just numbers.
> 5. **Not stating units** (Hz, kHz, dB) — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 mark per missing unit.
> 6. **Using $-20 \times n$ dB/decade** without explaining that each pole contributes independently — conceptual clarity matters.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Multistage Amplifiers**

- **Definition:** Cascade of ≥ 2 amplifier stages with overall gain = product (linear) = sum (dB).
- **Coupling types:** RC (most common in lab), Transformer (RF), Direct (DC).
- **Effect on Gain:** **Multiplicative in linear, Additive in dB.** A two-stage cascade with $A_1 = A_2 = 100$ gives $A_\text{total} = 10{,}000$ = **80 dB**.
- **Effect on Bandwidth:** **Shrinks** — upper cutoff decreases, lower cutoff increases.
- **Bandwidth shrinkage for $n$ identical stages:** $f_{Hn} = f_{H1}\sqrt{2^{1/n}-1}$.
- **Specific values:** $n=2 \Rightarrow 0.644\,f_{H1}$; $n=3 \Rightarrow 0.510\,f_{H1}$; $n=4 \Rightarrow 0.435\,f_{H1}$.
- **Gain–Bandwidth Product (GBP):** Approximately constant per transistor; the *system* GBP of a cascade is *much larger* than the single-stage GBP.
- **Roll-off slope:** $-20n$ dB/decade beyond $f_H$ (steeper for more stages).
- **Rise time formula:** $t_r = 0.35/f_H$ (single stage); $t_{r,\text{cascaded}} = 1.1\sqrt{t_{r1}^{2} + t_{r2}^{2}}$.
- **Lower cutoff for $n$ identical stages:** $f_{Ln} = f_{L1}/\sqrt{2^{1/n}-1}$ (note: **worsens** with cascading).
- **Lab measurement tip:** Use function generator at 1 kHz with $V_{in} = 50$ mV pp for mid-band gain; sweep 10 Hz → 1 MHz to find $f_L$ and $f_H$ at the −3 dB points.
- **KTU 2024 viva questions to prepare:**
  1. Why is RC coupling preferred over transformer coupling in audio amplifiers?
  2. What is the dominant pole? How does it limit the high-frequency response?
  3. Derive the bandwidth shrinkage factor for $n$ identical stages.
  4. What is the effect of cascading on the input and output impedance?
  5. Explain the gain–bandwidth trade-off with a practical example.
- **Practical pitfalls during lab:** Ground loops (use star grounding), oscilloscope probe compensation (calibrate before use), bias instability due to thermal runaway (use emitter resistors).
- **One-line summary:** ***"Cascade multiplies gain but shrinks bandwidth — every additional pole costs you 0.644 of the previous stage's bandwidth."***
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: MOSFET Amplifier (CS) - Frequency Response Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPL307 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** MOSFET Amplifier (CS) - Frequency Response Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Common Source (CS) MOSFET Amplifier** is a fundamental single-stage analog building block in which the **MOSFET terminal is common to both the input and output ports** for AC signals. The input signal is applied between the **Gate and Source**, and the amplified, phase-inverted output is taken between the **Drain and Source**.

The **Frequency Response** of this amplifier describes the variation of the **mid-band voltage gain ($A_v$)** as a function of the operating signal frequency. It is characterised by three distinct regions:

1. **Low-Frequency Region** — Gain rolls off due to external **coupling and bypass capacitors** ($C_{in}$, $C_{out}$, $C_S$).
2. **Mid-Band Region** — Gain is constant and maximum; the amplifier behaves as an ideal voltage-controlled voltage source.
3. **High-Frequency Region** — Gain rolls off due to **internal parasitic capacitances** of the MOSFET ($C_{gs}$, $C_{gd}$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> The two **3-dB cut-off frequencies** — lower cut-off ($f_L$) and upper cut-off ($f_H$) — define the **Bandwidth (BW)** of the amplifier:
> $$BW = f_H - f_L \approx f_H \quad (\text{since } f_H \gg f_L)$$

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Three-Speed Audio Speaker"

Imagine the MOSFET CS amplifier as a **high-fidelity audio amplifier** with three speed gears:

- 🎵 **Low Notes (Bass)** — *Coupling Capacitors*: The big coupling capacitors at input/output act like **slow-heating heavy speakers**. They cannot quickly pass DC and very low frequencies; they cause a "muffled bass" roll-off below $f_L$.
- 🎵 **Mid Notes (Vocals)** — *Mid-Band Region*: At voice frequencies (kHz range), the amplifier performs at its **peak, flat, faithful amplification** — just like a singer sounding clear and loud.
- 🎵 **High Notes (Treble)** — *Parasitic Capacitances*: The tiny internal MOSFET capacitances act like **stiff tweeters**. They short-circuit very high frequencies, causing a "shrill loss" roll-off above $f_H$.

The **usable audio band** of the amplifier is the flat region between $f_L$ and $f_H$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used:**
> - Permittivity of silicon: $\varepsilon_{si} = 11.7 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/m}$
> - Permittivity of silicon dioxide: $\varepsilon_{ox} = 3.9 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/m}$
> - $1 \text{ rad} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \text{ Hz}$ (frequency conversion)
> - Standard mid-band benchmark: $A_{v(mid)} = -g_m (R_D \Vert R_L)$

---

### 1.3 Block Diagram of the CS Amplifier (Conceptual)

```
       VGG
        |
        |
   +----+----+
   |         |
  Cin        |
   |         |
   +-- Gate  |
 Vi |   MOSFET  ---+--- Cout ---+--- Vo
   |         |     |          |
   +-- Source|     RD         RL
   |         |     |          |
   |        CS    GND        GND
   |         |
   +---------+
        RS
        |
       GND
       VDD ----+ 
```

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 DC Operating Point (Q-Point) Biasing

For a CS amplifier using **voltage-divider bias**, the gate is held at a fixed DC potential by the high-impedance divider network. The Q-point must be set in the **saturation region** for linear amplification.

$$V_{GS} = V_G - I_D R_S = V_G - K_n (V_{GS} - V_T)^2 R_S$$

$$I_{DQ} = K_n (V_{GSQ} - V_T)^2$$

$$V_{DSQ} = V_{DD} - I_{DQ}(R_D + R_S)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Saturation Condition:** $V_{DS} \geq V_{GS} - V_T$

### 2.2 Small-Signal AC Parameters

Once the Q-point is established, the MOSFET is replaced by its **small-signal hybrid-π model**:

$$g_m = \dfrac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{GS}} = 2 K_n (V_{GSQ} - V_T) = \sqrt{2 K_n I_{DQ}}$$

$$r_o = \dfrac{1}{\lambda I_{DQ}} \quad (\text{output resistance due to channel length modulation})$$

> For most low-frequency lab analysis, $r_o \gg R_D$ and is **neglected**.

### 2.3 Mid-Band Voltage Gain ($A_{v(mid)}$)

In the mid-band, all coupling and bypass capacitors act as **AC short-circuits**, and the parasitic capacitances act as **AC open-circuits**.

$$A_{v(mid)} = \dfrac{V_o}{V_i} = -g_m (R_D \Vert R_L) = -g_m R_{L}^{'}$$

The **negative sign** indicates a **180° phase inversion** between input and output.

### 2.4 Low-Frequency Response — The Three Pole Analysis

The lower cut-off frequency is determined by the **largest** time-constant among the three coupling/bypass capacitors:

**Pole due to Input Coupling Capacitor ($C_{in}$):**
$$f_{L1} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi (R_{sig} + R_{in}) C_{in}}$$

**Pole due to Output Coupling Capacitor ($C_{out}$):**
$$f_{L2} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi (R_D \Vert R_L) C_{out}}$$

**Pole due to Source Bypass Capacitor ($C_S$):**
$$f_{L3} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi (R_S \Vert \frac{1}{g_m}) C_S} \approx \dfrac{g_m}{2\pi C_S}$$ *(dominant in most designs)*

**Overall Lower Cut-off Frequency (approximate):**
$$f_L \approx \sqrt{f_{L1}^2 + f_{L2}^2 + f_{L3}^2 - 2(f_{L1}^2 f_{L2}^2 + f_{L2}^2 f_{L3}^2 + f_{L1}^2 f_{L3}^2) + f_{L1}^4 + f_{L2}^4 + f_{L3}^4}$$

For isolated, well-separated poles:
$$f_L \approx \max(f_{L1}, f_{L2}, f_{L3})$$

### 2.5 High-Frequency Response — Miller's Theorem

The upper cut-off frequency is dominated by **Miller multiplication** of $C_{gd}$:

**Miller's Theorem applied to $C_{gd}$:**
$$C_{gd,\text{Miller}} = C_{gd} (1 - A_v) \approx C_{gd} (1 + \vert A_{v(mid)} \vert)$$

**Effective input capacitance at the gate:**
$$C_{in,\text{eff}} = C_{gs} + C_{gd}(1 + \vert A_{v(mid)} \vert)$$

**Upper 3-dB Cut-off Frequency:**
$$f_H = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_{eq} C_{in,\text{eff}}}$$

where $R_{eq} = R_{sig} \Vert R_G$ (Thévenin resistance seen at the gate).

---

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Physical Meaning** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Transconductance $g_m$ | $g_m = 2K_n(V_{GSQ} - V_T) = \sqrt{2K_n I_{DQ}}$ | Siemens (S) or A/V | Voltage-to-current gain of MOSFET |
| Mid-band gain $A_v$ | $A_{v(mid)} = -g_m (R_D \Vert R_L)$ | dimensionless | Peak amplification in flat region |
| Lower pole (input) $f_{L1}$ | $f_{L1} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi (R_{sig} + R_{in}) C_{in}}$ | Hz | Cut-off due to input coupling cap |
| Lower pole (output) $f_{L2}$ | $f_{L2} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi (R_D \Vert R_L) C_{out}}$ | Hz | Cut-off due to output coupling cap |
| Lower pole (source) $f_{L3}$ | $f_{L3} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi (R_S \Vert 1/g_m) C_S}$ | Hz | Cut-off due to source bypass cap |
| Miller input cap $C_M$ | $C_M = C_{gd}(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$ | Farads (F) | Miller-multiplied feedback cap |
| Effective input cap | $C_{in,eff} = C_{gs} + C_{gd}(1+\vert A_v \vert)$ | Farads (F) | Total high-frequency load at gate |
| Upper cut-off $f_H$ | $f_H = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_{eq} C_{in,eff}}$ | Hz | Bandwidth limit due to parasitics |
| Bandwidth $BW$ | $BW = f_H - f_L \approx f_H$ | Hz | Usable amplification range |
| Gain-Bandwidth Product $GBW$ | $GBW = \vert A_{v(mid)} \vert \cdot f_H$ | Hz | Figure of merit of amplifier |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility of Frequency Response Analysis:**
> - **Audio amplifiers:** Determine whether the circuit can faithfully reproduce 20 Hz – 20 kHz.
> - **RF & Communication:** Set the channel bandwidth and selectivity.
> - **Operational amplifier design:** Direct CS stages determine open-loop $GBW$ of op-amps.
> - **Switched-capacitor filters:** Determine settling time and clock-rate limits.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Mid-Band Voltage Gain $A_{v(mid)}$

**Step 1:** Replace the MOSFET by its small-signal model. The drain current source is $g_m v_{gs}$.

**Step 2:** Apply KCL at the drain node (assuming $r_o \to \infty$):
$$v_o = -g_m v_{gs} (R_D \Vert R_L)$$

**Step 3:** Since the gate is **insulated** (no DC current flows), $v_{gs} = v_i$ (the full input voltage appears across $g$–$s$ terminals):
$$v_{gs} = v_i$$

**Step 4:** Substitute back:
$$v_o = -g_m v_i (R_D \Vert R_L)$$

**Step 5:** Take the ratio $v_o / v_i$:

$$\boxed{A_{v(mid)} = \dfrac{v_o}{v_i} = -g_m (R_D \Vert R_L)}$$

The negative sign indicates a **180° phase inversion** at mid-band.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Source-Bypass Capacitor Pole $f_{L3}$

This is the **dominant low-frequency pole** in most CS designs.

**Step 1:** Consider the small-signal model with the source resistor $R_S$ and bypass capacitor $C_S$ in parallel. The impedance is:
$$Z_S = R_S \Vert \dfrac{1}{sC_S} = \dfrac{R_S}{1 + sR_S C_S}$$

**Step 2:** Apply KVL from gate to source:
$$v_i = v_{gs} + g_m v_{gs} \cdot Z_S = v_{gs} (1 + g_m Z_S)$$

**Step 3:** Apply KCL at the drain:
$$v_o = -g_m v_{gs} (R_D \Vert R_L)$$

**Step 4:** Compute the transfer function:
$$A_v(s) = \dfrac{v_o}{v_i} = \dfrac{-g_m (R_D \Vert R_L)}{1 + g_m Z_S}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute $Z_S = \dfrac{R_S}{1 + sR_S C_S}$:

$$A_v(s) = \dfrac{-g_m (R_D \Vert R_L)}{1 + g_m \cdot \dfrac{R_S}{1 + sR_S C_S}}$$

$$A_v(s) = \dfrac{-g_m (R_D \Vert R_L)(1 + sR_S C_S)}{1 + sR_S C_S + g_m R_S}$$

**Step 6:** Factor the denominator to identify the pole:
$$1 + sR_S C_S + g_m R_S = 1 + s R_S C_S (1) + g_m R_S$$

Regrouping as $1 + sR_S C_S + g_m R_S = (1 + g_m R_S) \left(1 + s \dfrac{R_S C_S}{1 + g_m R_S}\right)$

**Step 7:** Identify the pole frequency:

$$\boxed{f_{L3} = \dfrac{1 + g_m R_S}{2\pi R_S C_S} \approx \dfrac{g_m}{2\pi C_S} \quad (\text{since } g_m R_S \gg 1)}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Upper Cut-off Frequency $f_H$ via Miller's Theorem

**Step 1:** The MOSFET has two internal capacitances:
- $C_{gs}$ : Gate-to-Source capacitance
- $C_{gd}$ : Gate-to-Drain capacitance (feedback)

**Step 2:** In the high-frequency small-signal model, $C_{gs}$ appears between gate and source (AC ground), and $C_{gd}$ appears between gate and drain.

**Step 3:** Apply **Miller's Theorem** to $C_{gd}$ (impedance between input and output):

Miller's theorem states: An impedance $Z$ between input node ($v_i$) and output node ($v_o$) can be split into:
- Input side: $\dfrac{Z}{1 - A_v}$
- Output side: $\dfrac{Z \cdot A_v}{1 - A_v}$

where $A_v = v_o / v_i$.

**Step 4:** Substituting $Z = 1/(sC_{gd})$ and $A_v = A_{v(mid)}$:
$$Z_{in} = \dfrac{1}{sC_{gd}(1 - A_{v(mid)})} = \dfrac{1}{sC_{gd}(1 + \vert A_{v(mid)} \vert)}$$

This is equivalent to a capacitance:
$$C_{gd,\text{Miller}} = C_{gd}(1 + \vert A_{v(mid)} \vert)$$

**Step 5:** The total effective input capacitance at the gate node is:
$$C_{in,eff} = C_{gs} + C_{gd}(1 + \vert A_{v(mid)} \vert)$$

**Step 6:** The Thévenin resistance seen at the gate from the source is:
$$R_{eq} = R_{sig} \Vert R_G$$

**Step 7:** The high-frequency response is a single-pole low-pass filter:
$$A_v(j\omega) = \dfrac{A_{v(mid)}}{1 + j\omega R_{eq} C_{in,eff}}$$

**Step 8:** The upper 3-dB cut-off frequency occurs when $\omega R_{eq} C_{in,eff} = 1$:

$$\boxed{f_H = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_{eq} C_{in,eff}} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi (R_{sig} \Vert R_G)(C_{gs} + C_{gd}(1+\vert A_{v(mid)} \vert))}}$$

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Lab-Style)

**Given circuit data:**
- $V_{DD} = 12 \text{ V}$, $R_D = 4.7 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_S = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$
- $K_n = 0.25 \text{ mA/V}^2$, $V_T = 2 \text{ V}$, $R_{sig} = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$
- $R_L = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, $C_{in} = 1 \mu\text{F}$, $C_{out} = 1 \mu\text{F}$, $C_S = 100 \mu\text{F}$
- $C_{gs} = 5 \text{ pF}$, $C_{gd} = 1 \text{ pF}$
- $R_G = 1 \text{ M}\Omega$ (gate bias resistor)
- Assume $I_{DQ} = 1 \text{ mA}$

**Step A — Compute $g_m$:**
$$g_m = \sqrt{2 K_n I_{DQ}} = \sqrt{2 \times 0.25 \times 10^{-3} \times 1 \times 10^{-3}} = 7.07 \times 10^{-4} \text{ S} = 0.707 \text{ mA/V}$$

**Step B — Compute mid-band gain:**
$$A_{v(mid)} = -g_m (R_D \Vert R_L) = -0.707 \times 10^{-3} \times (4700 \Vert 10000)$$
$$R_D \Vert R_L = \dfrac{4700 \times 10000}{4700 + 10000} = 3197 \Omega \approx 3.2 \text{ k}\Omega$$
$$A_{v(mid)} = -0.707 \times 3.2 = -2.26$$

**Step C — Lower cut-off frequencies:**
$$f_{L1} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi (R_{sig} + R_G) C_{in}} \approx \dfrac{1}{2\pi (10 \times 10^3)(1 \times 10^{-6})} = 15.9 \text{ Hz}$$

$$f_{L2} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi (R_D \Vert R_L) C_{out}} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi (3197)(1 \times 10^{-6})} = 49.8 \text{ Hz}$$

$$f_{L3} = \dfrac{g_m}{2\pi C_S} = \dfrac{0.707 \times 10^{-3}}{2\pi \times 100 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.125 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step D — Dominant lower cut-off:** $f_L \approx \max(f_{L1}, f_{L2}, f_{L3}) = 49.8 \text{ Hz} \approx 50 \text{ Hz}$

**Step E — Miller input capacitance:**
$$C_{in,eff} = C_{gs} + C_{gd}(1 + \vert A_{v(mid)} \vert) = 5 + 1 \times (1 + 2.26) = 8.26 \text{ pF}$$

**Step F — Upper cut-off frequency:**
$$R_{eq} = R_{sig} \Vert R_G = 10 \text{ k}\Omega \Vert 1 \text{ M}\Omega \approx 9.9 \text{ k}\Omega$$
$$f_H = \dfrac{1}{2\pi (9.9 \times 10^3)(8.26 \times 10^{-12})} = 1.95 \text{ MHz}$$

**Step G — Bandwidth and GBW:**
$$BW \approx f_H = 1.95 \text{ MHz}$$
$$GBW = \vert A_{v(mid)} \vert \times f_H = 2.26 \times 1.95 \times 10^6 = 4.4 \text{ MHz}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** The mid-band gain is only **2.26** because the bias is set to a low $I_{DQ}$. Increasing $I_{DQ}$ raises $g_m$ and hence $A_v$, but at the cost of reducing $f_H$ (Miller effect scales with $A_v$). This is the **classic gain–bandwidth trade-off** in CS amplifiers.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| **Region** | **Cause of Roll-off** | **Pole Equation** | **Strategy to Extend** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Low Frequency | Coupling caps ($C_{in}, C_{out}$) and source bypass $C_S$ | $f_L = 1/(2\pi R C)$ | Use larger $C_{in}, C_{out}, C_S$ |
| Mid-Band | None — flat response | $A_{v(mid)} = -g_m R_L^{'}$ | Increase $g_m$ or $R_L^{'}$ |
| High Frequency | MOSFET parasitic $C_{gs}, C_{gd}$ (Miller) | $f_H = 1/(2\pi R_{eq} C_{in,eff})$ | Use smaller MOSFET, cascode, or neutralisation |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Common Source Amplifier — Full Circuit Schematic (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[DC Supply VDD] --> B[DC Bias Network R1, R2]
    B --> C[Gate of MOSFET M1]
    D[AC Input Signal Vi] --> E[Coupling Capacitor Cin]
    E --> C
    C --> F[Gate-Source Capacitance Cgs]
    F --> G[Source of M1]
    G --> H[Source Resistor RS]
    H --> I[Source Bypass Capacitor CS to GND]
    G --> J[Drain of M1]
    A --> K[Drain Resistor RD]
    K --> J
    J --> L[Gate-Drain Capacitance Cgd - Miller]
    L --> C
    J --> M[Coupling Capacitor Cout]
    M --> N[Load Resistor RL]
    N --> O[AC Output Voltage Vo]
    O --> P[Oscilloscope / Spectrum Analyser]
    A --> Q[Function Generator to Vi]
```

---

### 4.2 Small-Signal Equivalent Model (Hybrid-π)

```mermaid
graph LR
    Sig[Signal Source Vsig] -->|Rsig| G[Gate Node]
    G -->|RG| GND1[AC Ground]
    G -->|Cgs| S[Source - AC Ground]
    G -->|Cgd| D[Drain Node]
    D -->|RD| GND2[AC Ground]
    D -->|RL| O[Output Vo]
    G -->|vgs| gm[gm vgs Current Source]
    gm --> D
```

---

### 4.3 Frequency Response — Bode Magnitude Plot

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[f L low frequency roll-off] -->|+20 dB/dec| B[Mid-Band Flat Region A v mid]
    B -->|-20 dB/dec| C[f H high frequency roll-off]
    A --> A1[Slope +20 dB/decade]
    B --> B1[Slope 0 dB/decade - Constant Gain]
    C --> C1[Slope -20 dB/decade]
    A -.->|3 dB below| A2[fL Lower Cut-off]
    C -.->|3 dB below| C2[fH Upper Cut-off]
    A2 --> B
    B --> C2
    A2 -.->|Bandwidth BW| C2
```

**Key points on the Bode plot:**

| **Marker** | **Frequency** | **Gain (dB)** | **Phase** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $f_L$ | Lower cut-off | $A_{v(mid)} - 3 \text{ dB}$ | +45° lead |
| $f_{mid}$ | Mid-band | $A_{v(mid)}$ (full) | 180° inversion |
| $f_H$ | Upper cut-off | $A_{v(mid)} - 3 \text{ dB}$ | -45° lag |

---

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Lab Measurement Setup

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Function Generator 1 kHz Sine] --> B[CS Amplifier Circuit on Breadboard]
    B --> C[Dual-Channel Oscilloscope]
    C --> D[Channel 1: Input Vi]
    C --> E[Channel 2: Output Vo]
    B --> F[DC Power Supply VDD = 12V]
    B --> G[Multimeter for VGSQ, IDQ]
    H[Sweep Frequency 10 Hz to 10 MHz] --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Procedure for Frequency Response Plot:**
> 1. Set input sine amplitude to **50 mV peak** (to keep the amplifier in linear region).
> 2. Vary frequency from **10 Hz to 10 MHz** in decade steps.
> 3. Record $V_o$ at each frequency and compute gain $= 20 \log_{10}(V_o/V_i)$.
> 4. Identify $f_L$ (where gain drops by 3 dB from mid-band) and $f_H$ (where gain drops by 3 dB on the high side).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define mid-band voltage gain of a Common Source MOSFET amplifier. Why is the gain constant in this region?**

**Model Answer:**

The **mid-band voltage gain** is the constant, maximum gain of the CS amplifier in the frequency range where all external coupling and bypass capacitors act as **AC short circuits** (low impedance) and all internal parasitic capacitances act as **AC open circuits** (high impedance). The mid-band gain is given by:
$$A_{v(mid)} = -g_m (R_D \Vert R_L)$$

The gain is **constant** in this region because none of the energy-storage elements (capacitors or inductors) introduce any frequency-dependent impedance variation. The amplifier behaves as a pure **resistive voltage-controlled current source** delivering maximum, flat amplification with a fixed **180° phase inversion**.

(3 marks)

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State Miller's theorem. How does it affect the high-frequency response of a CS amplifier?**

**Model Answer:**

**Miller's Theorem** states that an impedance $Z$ connected between two nodes with voltage gain $A_v$ between them can be replaced by two equivalent impedances to ground:
- At the input node: $Z_{in} = \dfrac{Z}{1 - A_v}$
- At the output node: $Z_{out} = \dfrac{Z \cdot A_v}{1 - A_v}$

For a CS amplifier, the feedback capacitance $C_{gd}$ between the gate and drain nodes is transformed (Miller-multiplied) into a much larger equivalent input capacitance:
$$C_{gd,\text{Miller}} = C_{gd} (1 + \vert A_{v(mid)} \vert)$$

This **drastically reduces the upper 3-dB cut-off frequency**:
$$f_H = \dfrac{1}{2\pi (R_{sig} \Vert R_G)(C_{gs} + C_{gd,\text{Miller}})}$$

For example, if $\vert A_v \vert = 10$ and $C_{gd} = 1 \text{ pF}$, the effective input capacitance increases by 11 pF — collapsing $f_H$ from MHz to tens of kHz.

(3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **mid-band voltage gain** of a CS MOSFET amplifier with a source bypass capacitor. Explain why the bypass capacitor is essential. (7 marks)

**(b)** A CS amplifier has $g_m = 2 \text{ mA/V}$, $R_D = 5 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_L = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, $C_{in} = 0.1 \mu\text{F}$, $C_{out} = 0.1 \mu\text{F}$, $C_S = 50 \mu\text{F}$, $R_{sig} = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$, and $R_G = 1 \text{ M}\Omega$. Calculate the **mid-band gain** and the **lower cut-off frequency** (assuming dominant-pole approximation). (7 marks)

**Model Solutions:**

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

**Step 1:** With the source bypass capacitor $C_S$ in place (acting as AC short at mid-band), the source terminal is at AC ground. The full input voltage appears between gate and source:
$$v_{gs} = v_i$$

**Step 2:** Applying KCL at the drain node, the small-signal drain current $g_m v_{gs}$ flows through the parallel combination of $R_D$ and $R_L$:
$$v_o = -g_m v_{gs} (R_D \Vert R_L)$$

**Step 3:** Substituting $v_{gs} = v_i$:
$$A_{v(mid)} = \dfrac{v_o}{v_i} = -g_m (R_D \Vert R_L)$$

**Why the bypass capacitor is essential:**
Without $C_S$, the source resistor $R_S$ introduces **series-series negative feedback** that reduces the gain by a factor $(1 + g_m R_S)$. The bypass capacitor $C_S$ provides a **low-impedance AC path** to ground, short-circuiting $R_S$ at signal frequencies and thus restoring the maximum mid-band gain. (7 marks)

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 — Mid-band gain:**
$$R_D \Vert R_L = \dfrac{5 \times 10}{5 + 10} = 3.33 \text{ k}\Omega$$
$$A_{v(mid)} = -g_m (R_D \Vert R_L) = -(2 \times 10^{-3})(3.33 \times 10^3) = -6.67$$
$$A_{v(mid)} \text{ in dB} = 20 \log_{10}(6.67) = 16.5 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 2 — Lower cut-off frequencies:**

$$f_{L1} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi (R_{sig} \Vert R_G) C_{in}} \approx \dfrac{1}{2\pi (1 \text{ k}\Omega)(0.1 \mu\text{F})} = 1592 \text{ Hz} \approx 1.6 \text{ kHz}$$

$$f_{L2} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi (R_D \Vert R_L) C_{out}} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi (3.33 \text{ k}\Omega)(0.1 \mu\text{F})} = 478 \text{ Hz}$$

$$f_{L3} = \dfrac{g_m}{2\pi C_S} = \dfrac{2 \times 10^{-3}}{2\pi (50 \times 10^{-6})} = 6.37 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 3 — Dominant pole:**
$$f_L = \max(f_{L1}, f_{L2}, f_{L3}) = f_{L1} = 1.6 \text{ kHz}$$

**Result:** $A_{v(mid)} = -6.67$ (or 16.5 dB), $f_L = 1.6 \text{ kHz}$ (7 marks)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of the small-signal high-frequency model, derive the expression for the **upper cut-off frequency $f_H$** of a CS MOSFET amplifier. State the role of **Miller's multiplication** in determining $f_H$. (7 marks)

**(b)** A CS amplifier has $C_{gs} = 4 \text{ pF}$, $C_{gd} = 1.5 \text{ pF}$, $R_{sig} = 600 \Omega$, $R_G = 1 \text{ M}\Omega$, and mid-band gain $A_{v(mid)} = -12$. Determine the **effective input capacitance**, **upper cut-off frequency $f_H$**, and the **gain-bandwidth product (GBW)**. Comment on the gain–bandwidth trade-off. (7 marks)

**Model Solutions:**

**Part (a) — Derivation:**
