**Step 1:** At high frequencies, the coupling and bypass capacitors remain AC short-circuits, but the internal parasitic capacitances $C_{gs}$ and $C_{gd}$ become significant.

**Step 2:** Using **Miller's theorem** on $C_{gd}$ (between gate and drain, with gain $A_v$):
$$C_{gd,\text{Miller}} = C_{gd}(1 + \vert A_{v(mid)} \vert)$$

**Step 3:** The total effective capacitance shunting the gate node is:
$$C_{in,eff} = C_{gs} + C_{gd}(1 + \vert A_{v(mid)} \vert)$$

**Step 4:** The Thévenin resistance at the gate is $R_{eq} = R_{sig} \Vert R_G$.

**Step 5:** The high-frequency transfer function is a single-pole low-pass filter:
$$A_v(j\omega) = \dfrac{A_{v(mid)}}{1 + j\omega R_{eq} C_{in,eff}}$$

**Step 6:** At $f_H$, $\omega R_{eq} C_{in,eff} = 1$:
$$f_H = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_{eq} C_{in,eff}} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi (R_{sig} \Vert R_G)(C_{gs} + C_{gd}(1+\vert A_{v(mid)} \vert))}$$

**Role of Miller's multiplication:** The factor $(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$ multiplies the small $C_{gd}$ into a large effective capacitance at the input, **drastically reducing $f_H$**. For a CS amplifier with $\vert A_v \vert = 10$ and $C_{gd} = 1 \text{ pF}$, the input side sees an extra 11 pF — this is why CS amplifiers have limited high-frequency response. (7 marks)

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 — Effective input capacitance:**
$$C_{in,eff} = C_{gs} + C_{gd}(1 + \vert A_{v(mid)} \vert) = 4 + 1.5 \times (1 + 12) = 4 + 19.5 = 23.5 \text{ pF}$$

**Step 2 — Upper cut-off frequency:**
$$R_{eq} = R_{sig} \Vert R_G = \dfrac{600 \times 1 \times 10^6}{600 + 1 \times 10^6} \approx 599.6 \Omega \approx 600 \Omega$$
$$f_H = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \times 600 \times 23.5 \times 10^{-12}} = \dfrac{1}{8.86 \times 10^{-8}} = 11.29 \text{ MHz}$$

**Step 3 — Gain-Bandwidth Product:**
$$GBW = \vert A_{v(mid)} \vert \times f_H = 12 \times 11.29 \times 10^6 = 135.5 \text{ MHz}$$

**Step 4 — Gain–Bandwidth Trade-off:**
If the gain is increased (e.g., to $\vert A_v \vert = 24$) by doubling $R_L^{'}$, the Miller multiplication factor becomes $(1 + 24) = 25$, so $C_{in,eff} = 4 + 37.5 = 41.5 \text{ pF}$, and $f_H$ drops to:
$$f_H^{'} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \times 600 \times 41.5 \times 10^{-12}} = 6.4 \text{ MHz}$$

The new $GBW = 24 \times 6.4 = 153.6 \text{ MHz}$ (slight increase). For a single-pole amplifier, **increasing $A_v$ by $N$ reduces $f_H$ by approximately the same factor $N$**, keeping $GBW$ approximately constant — this is the **fundamental gain–bandwidth trade-off**. (7 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Sign error trap:** Many students forget the **negative sign** of $A_{v(mid)} = -g_m R_L^{'}$. KTU examiners explicitly test this 180° phase inversion — losing **1 mark** if the sign is missing.
> - **Miller's sign confusion:** Miller's theorem gives $(1 - A_v)$, but for inverting amplifiers with $A_v < 0$, this becomes $(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$. Students often incorrectly write $(1 - A_v)$ literally, leading to a **smaller** effective capacitance and **wrong** $f_H$.
> - **Dominant pole misinterpretation:** The lower cut-off is **NOT** the sum of poles. Use $f_L \approx \max(f_{L1}, f_{L2}, f_{L3})$ for isolated poles, or the **standard approximation** $f_L \approx \sqrt{f_{L1}^2 + f_{L2}^2 + f_{L3}^2}$ only if poles are within a decade of each other. KTU 2024 answers **must** clearly state the dominant pole.
> - **Unit conversion trap:** $C_{gs}$, $C_{gd}$ in pF, $R$ in kΩ, $f_H$ in MHz — students frequently mix up the powers of 10. Always write out units explicitly.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

✅ **CS Amplifier Configuration:** Input at Gate, Output at Drain, Source is **common** to both.

✅ **Mid-band Gain:** $A_{v(mid)} = -g_m (R_D \Vert R_L)$ — **negative sign = 180° phase inversion**.

✅ **Transconductance:** $g_m = 2K_n(V_{GSQ} - V_T) = \sqrt{2K_n I_{DQ}}$.

✅ **Three Low-Frequency Poles:** $f_{L1}$ (input coupling), $f_{L2}$ (output coupling), $f_{L3}$ (source bypass — usually **dominant** if $C_S$ is small).

✅ **Source Bypass Capacitor $C_S$:** Restores gain by short-circuiting $R_S$ at AC; pole frequency $f_{L3} \approx g_m / (2\pi C_S)$.

✅ **Miller's Theorem:** $C_{gd}$ appears multiplied by $(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$ at the input.

✅ **Effective Input Capacitance:** $C_{in,eff} = C_{gs} + C_{gd}(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$ — this is what limits $f_H$.

✅ **Upper Cut-off Frequency:** $f_H = \dfrac{1}{2\pi (R_{sig} \Vert R_G) \cdot C_{in,eff}}$.

✅ **Bandwidth:** $BW = f_H - f_L \approx f_H$ (since $f_H \gg f_L$).

✅ **Gain–Bandwidth Product:** $GBW = \vert A_{v(mid)} \vert \cdot f_H$ — **roughly constant** for a single-stage CS amplifier.

✅ **Trade-off:** Higher mid-band gain ⇒ larger Miller multiplication ⇒ **lower** $f_H$ ⇒ **constant** $GBW$.

✅ **Lab Procedure Recap:**
   1. Set $V_{DD}$, bias the MOSFET in saturation, measure $V_{GSQ}, I_{DQ}$.
   2. Apply small AC sine (50 mV peak), vary frequency 10 Hz – 10 MHz.
   3. Record $V_o$, compute gain in dB, plot Bode plot.
   4. Identify $f_L$ and $f_H$ at the **-3 dB** points.
   5. Calculate $BW$ and $GBW$.

✅ **Typical Lab Values:** $A_{v(mid)} = 2$ to $20$ (linear) or $6$ to $26 \text{ dB}$, $f_L \sim 50$ Hz to $1$ kHz, $f_H \sim 1$ to $50$ MHz.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Feedback Amplifiers (Current Series, Voltage Series) - Gain and Frequency Response
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPL307 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Feedback Amplifiers (Current Series, Voltage Series) - Gain and Frequency Response

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Feedback Amplifier** is a multi-port electronic network in which a specified fraction of the output signal (voltage or current) is routed back to the input port and combined (in series or shunt) with the external source signal, forming a closed loop. The objective in the KTU 2024 lab module is to study the **two series-mixing topologies**: **Voltage-Series Feedback** (also called *series-shunt* or *voltage amplifier with series feedback*) and **Current-Series Feedback** (also called *series-series* or *transconductance amplifier*).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Series mixing always raises input resistance; voltage sampling lowers output resistance; current sampling raises output resistance.** These are non-negotiable consequences that KTU examiners use as a direct question every cycle.

Mathematically, a feedback amplifier is characterized by the **desensitivity factor** $\left(1 + A\beta\right)$, also called the *amount of feedback* or *return difference*. The closed-loop transfer function collapses to the universal form:

$$A_f = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}$$

where $A$ is the open-loop gain of the basic amplifier (without feedback) and $\beta$ is the reverse transfer function of the feedback network (assumed unilateral and load-independent).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine you are driving a car and trying to maintain **exactly 60 km/h** on a hilly road.

- **Without feedback (open loop):** You press the accelerator to a fixed position. On a downhill slope, the car speeds up; on an uphill slope, it slows down. The speed is *unstable* and *sensitive* to disturbances.
- **With feedback (closed loop):** You constantly *watch the speedometer* (sampling the output), compare it mentally with 60 km/h (mixing at the input), and adjust the accelerator accordingly. The car's actual speed stays near 60 km/h even when the road tilts.

| Car Analogy | Amplifier Equivalent |
|-------------|----------------------|
| Speedometer reading | Output signal being sampled |
| Your brain's comparison | Mixer / summing junction |
| Accelerator adjustment | Input error signal |
| 60 km/h target | Reference input |
| Driver skill | Loop gain $A\beta$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The "sacrifice" of feedback is that you can no longer command a *higher* speed by simply flooring the accelerator — the closed-loop speed (gain) is lower than the no-feedback top speed, but it is **stable, predictable, and disturbance-resistant**.

In the lab experiment, the *road disturbances* are replaced by **temperature drift, component tolerances, supply variations, and nonlinear transistor beta ($\beta_{transistor}$)**. The *driver's eyes* are the feedback resistors (e.g., $R_E$ unbypassed, or the collector-to-base resistor $R_f$).

## 1.3 Classification of the Four Canonical Topologies

The classification depends on **two independent binary choices** at the two ports:

| Quantity Sampled at Output | Quantity Mixed at Input | Topology Name | Idealized Gain | Best Realization |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Voltage | Series | **Voltage-Series** | $A_v$ (V/V) | **Emitter Follower**, Non-inverting Op-Amp, CE with $R_f$ from collector to base |
| Current | Series | **Current-Series** | $G_m$ (A/V) | **CE with unbypassed $R_E$** |
| Voltage | Shunt | Voltage-Shunt | $R_m$ (V/A) | Inverting Op-Amp, CE with $R_f$ from collector to base |
| Current | Shunt | Current-Shunt | $A_i$ (A/A) | CB with shunt output, current mirror |

The two topologies covered in this experiment are highlighted in **bold**.

## 1.4 Why These Two Topologies?

- **Voltage-Series Feedback** converts a *transconductance* device (BJT/MOSFET) into a near-ideal **voltage amplifier** with **high $R_{in}$** and **low $R_{out}$** — perfect for cascading.
- **Current-Series Feedback** converts the same device into a near-ideal **transconductance amplifier** with **high $R_{in}$** and **high $R_{out}$** — perfect for driving **low-impedance loads** where a stiff current is required (e.g., a headphone coil, a deflection yoke).

> [!TIP]
> **Exam heuristic:** Series mixing at the input = **voltage feedback**, so the feedback network and basic amplifier behave like a *voltage summer*. The mixing equation is KVL: $v_s = v_{\varepsilon} + v_f$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Generalized Feedback Block

A feedback amplifier, irrespective of topology, is decomposed into two functional blocks:

- **Basic Amplifier (A-block):** The forward path carrying signal from source toward load, with gain $A$.
- **Feedback Network (β-block):** A passive reciprocal network that senses the output and produces a feedback signal $x_f = \beta \cdot x_o$.

The two ports interact as follows:

1. **Input port mixing:** $x_s$, $x_{\varepsilon}$, and $x_f$ are related by the chosen mixing law (KVL for series, KCL for shunt).
2. **Output port sampling:** $x_o$ is sensed as a voltage across the load (voltage sampling) or a current through the load (current sampling).

> [!NOTE]
> The *loading effect* of the β-network on both the input and output of the A-block is absorbed into $A$ itself (this is the standard **"two-port loading"** approach adopted by KTU 2024 syllabus).

## 2.2 The Four Cardinal Effects of Negative Feedback

For negative feedback ($\,1 + A\beta > 1\,$), **four** properties change in a mathematically predictable manner:

1. **Gain Desensitization:** Closed-loop gain depends on passive components only.
2. **Bandwidth Extension:** Upper cutoff rises; lower cutoff falls; **gain-bandwidth product is invariant.**
3. **Impedance Reshaping:** $R_{in}$ and $R_{out}$ move toward *ideal* values for the chosen amplifier type.
4. **Linearity and SNR:** Harmonic distortion and internal noise are reduced by the same factor $1 + A\beta$.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Cheat Sheet)

> [!WARNING]
> **Table-formatting rule (read first!):** Inside this markdown table, the absolute-value symbol is rendered as `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent the markdown parser from breaking the row. Copy the LaTeX source directly.

| # | Quantity | Open-Loop Form | Closed-Loop Form (Series Mixing) | Closed-Loop Form (Shunt Mixing) | Units |
|---|----------|----------------|----------------------------------|--------------------------------|-------|
| 1 | Closed-loop gain | $A$ | $A_f = A \big/ (1 + A\beta)$ | $A_f = A \big/ (1 + A\beta)$ | depends on topology |
| 2 | Desensitivity factor | $1$ | $1 + A\beta$ | $1 + A\beta$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | Loop gain | $0$ | $A\beta$ | $A\beta$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | Input resistance $R_{in,f}$ | $R_{in}$ | $R_{in} \cdot (1 + A\beta)$ | $R_{in} \big/ (1 + A\beta)$ | $\Omega$ |
| 5 | Output resistance $R_{out,f}$ | $R_{out}$ | $R_{out} \big/ (1 + A\beta)$ (voltage sampling) | $R_{out} \cdot (1 + A\beta)$ (current sampling) | $\Omega$ |
| 6 | Upper cutoff $f_{H,f}$ | $f_H$ | $f_H \cdot (1 + A\beta)$ | $f_H \cdot (1 + A\beta)$ | Hz |
| 7 | Lower cutoff $f_{L,f}$ | $f_L$ | $f_L \big/ (1 + A\beta)$ | $f_L \big/ (1 + A\beta)$ | Hz |
| 8 | Bandwidth $BW_f$ | $BW$ | $BW \cdot (1 + A\beta)$ | $BW \cdot (1 + A\beta)$ | Hz |
| 9 | Gain-Bandwidth Product | $GBP$ | $GBP$ (conserved) | $GBP$ (conserved) | Hz |
| 10 | Feedback factor (CE with $R_E$) | — | $\beta \approx R_E \big/ R_C$ | — | dimensionless |
| 11 | Feedback factor (CE with $R_f$ C↔B) | — | $\beta \approx 1 \big/ (1 + R_f \big/ R_C)$ | — | dimensionless |
| 12 | Feedback factor (Emitter Follower) | — | $\beta \approx 1$ | — | dimensionless |

## 2.4 Topology-Specific Identification Rules

| Topology | Sample Element | Mix Element | $R_{in}$ | $R_{out}$ | Ideal Source | Ideal Load |
|----------|---------------|-------------|----------|-----------|--------------|------------|
| Voltage-Series | $R_f$ across load (voltage divider) | $R_f$ in series with input (KVL) | **Increases** | **Decreases** | Voltage source $v_s$ | High $R_L$ |
| Current-Series | $R_E$ in series with output device | $R_E$ in series with input loop | **Increases** | **Increases** | Voltage source $v_s$ | Low $R_L$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Series-mixing memory hook:** *Both* voltage-series and current-series feedback **raise $R_{in}$**. The discriminator is the output: voltage-series **lowers $R_{out}$** while current-series **raises $R_{out}$**.

## 2.5 Why Engineers Need This in Practice

- **Cable driving:** Voltage-series feedback turns a lossy cable (capacitive load) into a benign 50 Ω/75 Ω source.
- **Audio preamplifiers:** Current-series feedback provides a current-source-like output that flattens the response of a moving-coil loudspeaker.
- **Instrumentation:** The high $R_{in}$ from series feedback is essential when the source is a Wheatstone bridge or a piezoelectric transducer (where loading would corrupt the reading).
- **Process control / regulators:** The 7805/7812 series of linear regulators are textbook *current-series* (and *current-shunt*) feedback amplifiers with discrete op-amp error amplifiers.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Universal Closed-Loop Gain

**Given:** A basic amplifier with open-loop gain $A$ and a feedback network with transfer ratio $\beta$, where the input port mixes such that the *error signal* $x_{\varepsilon}$ is the difference between source $x_s$ and feedback $x_f$.

**Step 1 — Define the forward path.** The amplifier output is proportional to the error signal:

$$x_o = A \cdot x_{\varepsilon}$$

**Step 2 — Define the feedback path.** The feedback network output is proportional to the sampled output:

$$x_f = \beta \cdot x_o$$

**Step 3 — Apply the input mixing law (series-KVL for our two topologies):**

$$x_{\varepsilon} = x_s - x_f = x_s - \beta \cdot x_o$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Step 3 into Step 1:**

$$x_o = A \left( x_s - \beta \cdot x_o \right)$$

**Step 5 — Distribute $A$ on the right-hand side:**

$$x_o = A \cdot x_s - A \beta \cdot x_o$$

**Step 6 — Collect the $x_o$ terms on the left-hand side:**

$$x_o + A\beta \cdot x_o = A \cdot x_s$$

**Step 7 — Factor out $x_o$:**

$$x_o \left( 1 + A\beta \right) = A \cdot x_s$$

**Step 8 — Solve for the closed-loop gain $A_f \triangleq x_o / x_s$:**

$$A_f = \frac{x_o}{x_s} = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Q.E.D.** This is the **single most important equation** in the entire KTU analog syllabus. Every derivation below is a consequence of it.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Input Resistance with Series Mixing (KVL Port)

**Given:** Series-mixed input port where the source voltage $v_s$ is split between the amplifier's intrinsic input resistance $R_{in}$ and the feedback voltage $v_f$ delivered by the β-network.

**Step 1 — Apply KVL around the input loop:**

$$v_s = v_{\varepsilon} + v_f$$

**Step 2 — Recognize that the current entering the amplifier's intrinsic input is:**

$$i_s = \frac{v_{\varepsilon}}{R_{in}}$$

**Step 3 — Recognize the feedback signal as a sampled fraction of the output:**

$$v_f = \beta \cdot x_o$$

But because the A-block converts $v_{\varepsilon}$ to $x_o$ with gain $A$, we have $x_o = A \cdot v_{\varepsilon}$. Substituting:

$$v_f = \beta \cdot A \cdot v_{\varepsilon} = A\beta \cdot v_{\varepsilon}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Step 3 into Step 1:**

$$v_s = v_{\varepsilon} + A\beta \cdot v_{\varepsilon} = v_{\varepsilon} \left( 1 + A\beta \right)$$

**Step 5 — Write the *apparent* input resistance seen by the source $v_s$:**

$$R_{in,f} \triangleq \frac{v_s}{i_s} = \frac{v_{\varepsilon} \left( 1 + A\beta \right)}{v_{\varepsilon} \big/ R_{in}} = R_{in} \left( 1 + A\beta \right)$$

**Result:**

$$\boxed{R_{in,f} = R_{in} \left( 1 + A\beta \right)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is **why** voltage-series and current-series feedback both raise input impedance — the β-network *opposes* changes in $v_{\varepsilon}$ by feeding back a cancelling voltage, demanding more drive current from the source to overcome the bootstrap effect.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Output Resistance — Voltage Sampling (Test-Source Method)

**Given:** Voltage-sampled output port. To find $R_{out,f}$, we zero the independent source ($v_s = 0$), apply a test voltage $v_t$ at the output, and measure the resulting current $i_t$.

**Step 1 — With the source killed, the feedback network delivers a feedback voltage:**

$$v_f = \beta \cdot v_t$$

**Step 2 — The error signal (input to A-block) becomes:**

$$v_{\varepsilon} = 0 - v_f = -\beta \cdot v_t$$

**Step 3 — The A-block output is:**

$$v_{o,A} = A \cdot v_{\varepsilon} = -A\beta \cdot v_t$$

**Step 4 — Apply KCL at the output node:** the test current $i_t$ splits between the A-block's intrinsic output resistance $R_{out}$ and the feedback network's loading resistance $R_{11,\beta}$ (assumed absorbed in $R_{out}$):

$$i_t = \frac{v_t - v_{o,A}}{R_{out}} = \frac{v_t - \left( -A\beta v_t \right)}{R_{out}} = \frac{v_t \left( 1 + A\beta \right)}{R_{out}}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the closed-loop output resistance:**

$$R_{out,f} = \frac{v_t}{i_t} = \frac{R_{out}}{1 + A\beta}$$

**Result:**

$$\boxed{R_{out,f,\text{voltage-sampled}} = \frac{R_{out}}{1 + A\beta}}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Output Resistance — Current Sampling (Test-Source Method)

**Given:** Current-sampled output port. The β-network sits in series with the load and senses $i_L$. Apply a test current $i_t$ at the output with $v_s = 0$.

**Step 1 — The feedback signal (which is a *voltage* in series-mixing) becomes:**

$$v_f = \beta \cdot i_L = \beta \cdot i_t \quad \text{(at the test port)}$$

**Step 2 — The A-block output current produced in response:**

$$i_{o,A} = A \cdot v_{\varepsilon} = A \cdot \left( - v_f \right) = -A\beta \cdot i_t$$

**Step 3 — Apply KVL at the output node (test current injected):**

$$v_t = \left( i_t - i_{o,A} \right) R_{out} = \left( i_t + A\beta \, i_t \right) R_{out} = i_t \, R_{out} \left( 1 + A\beta \right)$$

**Step 4 — Solve:**

$$R_{out,f} = \frac{v_t}{i_t} = R_{out} \left( 1 + A\beta \right)$$

**Result:**

$$\boxed{R_{out,f,\text{current-sampled}} = R_{out} \left( 1 + A\beta \right)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Compare the two boxed equations: voltage sampling *divides* $R_{out}$ by $(1 + A\beta)$, while current sampling *multiplies* it by $(1 + A\beta)$. **Always check the sampling convention before writing $R_{out,f}$ in your exam script.**

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Bandwidth Extension and GBP Invariance

**Given:** A single-pole basic amplifier with midband gain $A_{\text{mid}}$ and upper cutoff $\omega_H = 2\pi f_H$:

$$A(s) = \frac{A_{\text{mid}}}{1 + s / \omega_H}$$

**Step 1 — Substitute into the universal feedback equation:**

$$A_f(s) = \frac{A(s)}{1 + A(s)\beta}$$

**Step 2 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $(1 + s/\omega_H)$:**

$$A_f(s) = \frac{A_{\text{mid}}}{\left( 1 + s / \omega_H \right) + A_{\text{mid}} \beta \left( 1 + s / \omega_H \right)}$$

**Step 3 — Factor the denominator:**

$$A_f(s) = \frac{A_{\text{mid}}}{\left( 1 + A_{\text{mid}} \beta \right) \left[ 1 + \dfrac{s}{\omega_H \left( 1 + A_{\text{mid}} \beta \right)} \right]}$$

**Step 4 — Identify the new midband gain and new upper cutoff:**

$$A_{f,\text{mid}} = \frac{A_{\text{mid}}}{1 + A_{\text{mid}}\beta}, \qquad \omega_{H,f} = \omega_H \left( 1 + A_{\text{mid}}\beta \right)$$

**Step 5 — Compute the gain-bandwidth product of the closed-loop amplifier:**

$$\text{GBP}_f = A_{f,\text{mid}} \cdot \omega_{H,f} = \frac{A_{\text{mid}}}{1 + A_{\text{mid}}\beta} \cdot \omega_H \left( 1 + A_{\text{mid}}\beta \right) = A_{\text{mid}} \cdot \omega_H = \text{GBP}_{\text{open-loop}}$$

**Result:**

$$\boxed{\text{GBP}_f = \text{GBP}_{\text{open-loop}} = \text{constant}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical conclusion:** When you trade gain for bandwidth, the **GBP stays constant**. Designers use this to design wideband amplifiers by deliberately *under-driving* the open-loop gain.

## 3.6 Derivation 6: Closed-Loop Gain of a CE Stage with Unbypassed $R_E$ (Current-Series)

**Given:** Common-emitter BJT with collector resistor $R_C$, unbypassed emitter resistor $R_E$, small-signal parameters $r_\pi$, $g_m$, $r_o \to \infty$.

**Step 1 — Intrinsic voltage gain (open-loop, with $R_E$ bypassed by $C_E$):**

$$A = -g_m R_C$$

**Step 2 — Intrinsic transresistance feedback factor (current sampled through $R_E$, voltage mixed in series):**

The emitter current $i_E \approx i_C = -v_o / R_C$ (sign convention: $v_o$ drops positive at collector).

The feedback voltage delivered to the input loop is $v_f = i_E \cdot R_E \approx - v_o R_E / R_C$.

The feedback factor is therefore $\beta_f = v_f / v_o = - R_E / R_C$ (with proper sign convention for negative feedback, we take the magnitude):

$$\vert \beta \vert = \frac{R_E}{R_C}$$

**Step 3 — Loop gain $A\beta$:**

$$A \beta = \left( -g_m R_C \right) \cdot \left( - \frac{R_E}{R_C} \right) = g_m R_E$$

**Step 4 — Apply the universal feedback equation:**

$$A_f = \frac{-g_m R_C}{1 + g_m R_E}$$

**Step 5 — Approximate for $g_m R_E \gg 1$:**

$$A_f \approx \frac{-g_m R_C}{g_m R_E} = -\frac{R_C}{R_E}$$

**Result:**

$$\boxed{A_f \approx - \frac{R_C}{R_E}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The closed-loop gain now depends only on the *ratio of two passive resistors*, eliminating the temperature-sensitive $g_m$ term. This is the KTU-board's favourite example of *gain desensitization*.

## 3.7 Derivation 7: Closed-Loop Input Resistance of the Same Stage

**Step 1 — Intrinsic input resistance looking into the base (without $R_E$):**

$$R_{in} = r_\pi = \frac{\beta_{\text{BJT}}}{g_m}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the series-mixing input-resistance formula:**

$$R_{in,f} = R_{in} \left( 1 + A\beta \right) = r_\pi \left( 1 + g_m R_E \right)$$

**Step 3 — Approximate for $g_m R_E \gg 1$ and using $r_\pi g_m = \beta_{\text{BJT}}$:**

$$R_{in,f} \approx r_\pi \cdot g_m R_E = \beta_{\text{BJT}} R_E$$

**Result:**

$$\boxed{R_{in,f} \approx \beta_{\text{BJT}} R_E}$$

> [!TIP]
> This explains *why* a high-impedance microphone preamp uses an unbypassed $R_E$: the input resistance is *boosted* by the current gain of the transistor.

## 3.8 Derivation 8: Closed-Loop Output Resistance (Current Sampling)

**Step 1 — Intrinsic $R_{out} \approx R_C$ (assuming $r_o \to \infty$).**

**Step 2 — Apply the current-sampling output-resistance formula:**

$$R_{out,f} = R_{out} \left( 1 + A\beta \right) = R_C \left( 1 + g_m R_E \right)$$

**Step 3 — Approximate for $g_m R_E \gg 1$:**

$$R_{out,f} \approx g_m R_C R_E$$

**Result:**

$$\boxed{R_{out,f} \approx g_m R_C R_E \quad \text{(very large)}}$$

This high $R_{out}$ is the *signature* of current-series feedback: the amplifier behaves like a current source.

## 3.9 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Parameter | Voltage-Series (CE with C↔B $R_f$) | Current-Series (CE with unbypassed $R_E$) |
|-----------|-------------------------------------|---------------------------------------------|
| Open-loop gain $A$ | $-g_m R_C$ | $-g_m R_C$ |
| Feedback factor $\beta$ | $\approx 1/(1 + R_f/R_C)$ | $R_E/R_C$ |
| Loop gain $A\beta$ | large | $g_m R_E$ |
| $A_f$ | $A/(1+A\beta)$ | $\approx -R_C/R_E$ |
| $R_{in,f}$ | $R_{in}(1+A\beta)$ ↑↑ | $\beta_{\text{BJT}} R_E$ ↑↑ |
| $R_{out,f}$ | $R_C/(1+A\beta)$ ↓ | $R_C(1+A\beta)$ ↑ |
| $f_{H,f}$ | $f_H(1+A\beta)$ ↑ | $f_H(1+A\beta)$ ↑ |
| Best for | Voltage amplification, cable drive | Transconductance, low-$R_L$ loads |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of a Generalized Series-Mixing Feedback Amplifier

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the *functional flow* of a generic series-mixing feedback amplifier. The β-block senses the output and injects a voltage in series with the source.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcNode["Source v_s"] --> serMixA(("+"))
    serMixB(("−")) --> serMixA
    serMixA --> ampIn["A-Block Input v_eps"]
    ampIn --> ampBody["Basic Amplifier (Gain A)"]
    ampBody --> ampOut["Output x_o"]
    ampOut --> loadNode["Load R_L"]
    ampOut --> fbTap["Sampling Point"]
    fbTap --> fbNet["Feedback Network (Beta)"]
    fbNet --> serMixB
    classDef srcStyle fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e,color:#000
    classDef ampStyle fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#000
    classDef fbStyle fill:#fecaca,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#000
    classDef mixStyle fill:#d9f99d,stroke:#365314,color:#000
    class srcNode srcStyle
    class ampBody,ampIn,ampOut,loadNode ampStyle
    class fbNet,fbTap fbStyle
    class serMixA,serMixB mixStyle
```

## 4.2 Decision Tree for Identifying the Four Topologies

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode(["Start: Classify Amplifier"]) --> q1{"What is sampled<br/>at output?"}
    q1 -- "Voltage<br/>(across R_L)" --> vPath["Voltage-Sampled Branch"]
    q1 -- "Current<br/>(through R_L)" --> iPath["Current-Sampled Branch"]
    vPath --> q2{"What is mixed<br/>at input?"}
    q2 -- "Series<br/>(KVL)" --> vs["VOLTAGE-SERIES<br/>R_in UP, R_out DOWN"]
    q2 -- "Shunt<br/>(KCL)" --> vsh["VOLTAGE-SHUNT<br/>R_in DOWN, R_out DOWN"]
    iPath --> q3{"What is mixed<br/>at input?"}
    q3 -- "Series<br/>(KVL)" --> cs["CURRENT-SERIES<br/>R_in UP, R_out UP"]
    q3 -- "Shunt<br/>(KCL)" --> ish["CURRENT-SHUNT<br/>R_in DOWN, R_out UP"]
    classDef hi fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534,color:#000
    class vs,cs hi
```

## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram of Voltage-Series Feedback (Emitter Follower Realization)

The emitter follower is the textbook voltage-series stage. The β-network is just the voltage divider formed by $Z_E$ and the source resistance.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    vb["v_s"] --> sumA(("+"))
    sumA --> betaBlock["Beta Network<br/>beta approx 1<br/>(unity-gain buffer)"]
    betaBlock --> ampBlockA["A-Block<br/>A = g_m * r_pi / 1<br/>approx large"]
    ampBlockA --> voA["v_o at Emitter"]
    voA --> loadA["R_L to ground"]
    voA --> fbA["Sample v_o"]
    fbA --> sumA
    voA --> summB(("−"))
    summB --> corrLoop["Loop Gain A*beta = g_m * Z_E"]
    classDef voStyle fill:#bae6fd,stroke:#075985,color:#000
    classDef sumStyle fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e,color:#000
    class vb,voA,loadA,fbA voStyle
    class sumA,summB sumStyle
```

**Key feature:** $A_f \approx 1$, $R_{in,f} = r_\pi (1 + g_m Z_E) \approx \beta_{\text{BJT}} Z_E$, $R_{out,f} = \dfrac{r_e + R_s/\beta_{\text{BJT}}}{1} \approx r_e$ (very small).

## 4.4 Functional Block Diagram of Current-Series Feedback (CE with Unbypassed $R_E$)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    vinB["v_s"] --> serMixC(("+"))
    serMixC --> rPiNode["r_pi<br/>Input Resistance"]
    rPiNode --> gmStage["g_m v_be<br/>Current Source"]
    gmStage --> rcNode["R_C<br/>Output Resistance"]
    rcNode --> voutB["v_o at Collector"]
    voutB --> loadB["R_L to ground"]
    gmStage --> reNode["R_E Unbypassed<br/>Senses i_E approx i_C"]
    reNode --> betaOut["beta approx R_E / R_C<br/>Current-Series Sampling"]
    betaOut --> serMixC
    voutB --> cbSamp["Current Sampling<br/>i_C = -v_o / R_C"]
    cbSamp --> betaOut
    classDef ceStyle fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534,color:#000
    classDef fbStyle fill:#fecaca,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#000
    class vinB,rPiNode,gmStage,rcNode,voutB,loadB ceStyle
    class reNode,betaOut,cbSamp fbStyle
```

## 4.5 Frequency-Response Sketches (Conceptual)

| Stage | Lower Cutoff | Midband | Upper Cutoff |
|-------|--------------|---------|--------------|
| Without feedback | $f_L$ | $A_{\text{mid}}$ (dB) | $f_H$ |
| With feedback | $f_L / (1+A\beta)$ | $A_{\text{mid}}/(1+A\beta)$ (dB) | $f_H (1+A\beta)$ |

**Gain (dB) vs log(f):** The closed-loop curve is the open-loop curve *vertically shifted downward* by $20\log_{10}(1+A\beta)$ and *horizontally stretched* outward by the same factor — keeping the roll-off slope (–20 dB/decade for a single pole) and the **GBP invariant** at every point on the asymptote.

## 4.6 Equivalent Two-Port View (KTU Lab Realization Hint)

For the lab circuit, the BJT small-signal model *with* the unbypassed $R_E$ is the *complete* A-block, while the β-network is *implicit* in the way $R_E$ simultaneously samples current and feeds voltage. Therefore:

- **Voltage-Series (CE with C↔B $R_f$):** Use the formula $A_f \approx -R_C / R_E$ only if $R_f$ is interpreted as an equivalent emitter resistor after the Miller transformation.
- **Current-Series (CE with $R_E$):** Direct application of Derivation 6.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfalls that consistently cost marks in KTU 2024 boards:**
> 1. **Sign error in $\beta$** — forgetting that negative feedback demands a $180°$ phase inversion between $x_o$ and $x_f$. Many students write $\beta = +R_E/R_C$ and end up with positive feedback.
> 2. **Confusing $R_{out,f}$ formula** — applying the voltage-sampling formula $R_{out}/(1+A\beta)$ to a current-sampled circuit (or vice-versa). The single-line table in §2.3 must be memorized.
> 3. **Skipping units** — KTU examiners deduct **half a mark** for every formula written without its unit.
> 4. **Forgetting GBP invariance** — students often answer "bandwidth increases and so does GBP." This is **wrong**; GBP is constant.
> 5. **Not drawing arrows on Mermaid / block diagrams** — examiners want explicit *direction* of signal flow. Always use → or labelled arrows.
> 6. **Mixing up "amount of feedback"** — the amount of feedback is $(1+A\beta)$, *not* $A\beta$ (which is loop gain). Two different quantities.

## 5.2 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper 2]
**Define negative feedback. List the four basic feedback topologies and state the effect of negative feedback on input and output resistances in each case.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Negative feedback is the process of feeding back a portion of the output signal to the input in such a way that it *opposes* the original input signal, reducing the net error and stabilizing the closed-loop gain.

**Four topologies (1 mark):** Voltage-Series, Voltage-Shunt, Current-Series, Current-Shunt.

**Resistance effects (1 mark):**
- Voltage-Series → $R_{in}$ increases, $R_{out}$ decreases.
- Voltage-Shunt → $R_{in}$ decreases, $R_{out}$ decreases.
- Current-Series → $R_{in}$ increases, $R_{out}$ increases.
- Current-Shunt → $R_{in}$ decreases, $R_{out}$ increases.

### Question A2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is the effect of negative feedback on the bandwidth and gain-bandwidth product of an amplifier?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The bandwidth $BW_f$ of a feedback amplifier **increases** by a factor of $(1 + A\beta)$ compared to the open-loop bandwidth $BW$, i.e., $BW_f = BW \cdot (1 + A\beta)$. However, the **midband gain decreases** by the same factor: $A_{f,\text{mid}} = A_{\text{mid}} / (1 + A\beta)$. The product $A_{f,\text{mid}} \times BW_f = A_{\text{mid}} \times BW$ is **invariant**, meaning the gain-bandwidth product (GBP) of the amplifier is **not** affected by negative feedback. **(1 mark each for the three statements: BW↑, gain↓, GBP invariant.)**

---

## 5.3 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question B-Option-A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a) [7 marks]** For a CE amplifier with an unbypassed emitter resistor $R_E$, derive the expressions for (i) closed-loop voltage gain, (ii) closed-loop input resistance, and (iii) closed-loop output resistance. Identify the feedback topology.

**(b) [7 marks)** With $R_C = 4.7\ \text{k}\Omega$, $R_E = 470\ \Omega$, $\beta_{\text{BJT}} = 100$, $V_{CC} = 12\ \text{V}$, $r_o \to \infty$, $V_T = 26\ \text{mV}$, and quiescent collector current $I_C = 1.2\ \text{mA}$, calculate (i) $g_m$, (ii) loop gain $A\beta$, (iii) closed-loop voltage gain $A_f$, (iv) closed-loop input resistance $R_{in,f}$, and (v) closed-loop output resistance $R_{out,f}$. Comment on the type of feedback.

#### Model Solution to B-Option-A

**(a) Part (i) — Closed-loop voltage gain:** As derived in §3.6,

$$A_f = \frac{-g_m R_C}{1 + g_m R_E} \approx -\frac{R_C}{R_E}$$

**(a) Part (ii) — Closed-loop input resistance:** As derived in §3.7,

$$R_{in,f} = r_\pi (1 + g_m R_E) \approx \beta_{\text{BJT}} R_E$$

**(a) Part (iii) — Closed-loop output resistance:** As derived in §3.8,

$$R_{out,f} = R_C (1 + g_m R_E)$$

**Topology identification:** $R_E$ samples the *emitter (output) current* and feeds back a *voltage* in *series* with the input. This is a **Current-Series Feedback** amplifier (also called *series-series*). **(1 mark for identification.)**

**(b) Numerical computation:**

**(i) Transconductance $g_m$:**

$$g_m = \frac{I_C}{V_T} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{-3}}{26 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.04615\ \text{S} = 46.15\ \text{mA/V}$$

**(ii) Loop gain $A\beta$:**

$$A\beta = g_m R_E = 0.04615 \times 470 = 21.69$$

**(iii) Closed-loop voltage gain:**

$$A_f = \frac{-g_m R_C}{1 + g_m R_E} = \frac{-0.04615 \times 4700}{1 + 21.69} = \frac{-216.9}{22.69} = -9.56\ \text{V/V}$$

$$A_f \approx -\frac{R_C}{R_E} = -\frac{4700}{470} = -10.00\ \text{V/V} \quad \text{(check)}$$ 

**(iv) Closed-loop input resistance:**

$$r_\pi = \frac{\beta_{\text{BJT}}}{g_m} = \frac{100}{0.04615} = 2166.7\ \Omega \approx 2.17\ \text{k}\Omega$$

$$R_{in,f} = r_\pi (1 + g_m R_E) = 2166.7 \times 22.69 = 49{,}164\ \Omega \approx 49.16\ \text{k}\Omega$$

$$R_{in,f} \approx \beta_{\text{BJT}} R_E = 100 \times 470 = 47{,}000\ \Omega = 47\ \text{k}\Omega \quad \text{(check)}$$

**(v) Closed-loop output resistance:**

$$R_{out,f} = R_C (1 + g_m R_E) = 4700 \times 22.69 = 106{,}643\ \Omega \approx 106.6\ \text{k}\Omega$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Topology confirmation:** $R_{in,f} = 47\ \text{k}\Omega \gg R_{in}$ (open-loop, $2.17\ \text{k}\Omega$), and $R_{out,f} = 106.6\ \text{k}\Omega \gg R_C$ (open-loop, $4.7\ \text{k}\Omega$). Both $R_{in}$ and $R_{out}$ *increased* → **Current-Series Feedback** confirmed.

---

### Question B-Option-B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the expression for the closed-loop gain of a generalized feedback amplifier starting from the input summing equation. Show that the closed-loop gain depends only on the feedback network when the loop gain $A\beta$ is very large. List any **three** advantages and **two** disadvantages of negative feedback.

**(b) [7 marks]** An amplifier without feedback has a midband gain of $A_{\text{mid}} = 2000$, lower cutoff $f_L = 50\ \text{Hz}$, and upper cutoff $f_H = 25\ \text{kHz}$. If a feedback network with $\beta = 0.04$ is applied (negative feedback), compute the new midband gain, new bandwidth, and new cutoffs. Verify GBP invariance.

#### Model Solution to B-Option-B

**(a) Derivation (3 marks):** See Derivation 1 in §3.1. The closed-loop gain is $A_f = A / (1 + A\beta)$.

When $A\beta \gg 1$:

$$A_f \approx \frac{A}{A\beta} = \frac{1}{\beta}$$

which depends **only** on the passive feedback network. **(1 mark)**

**Three advantages (1.5 marks, 0.5 each):**
1. Gain stability against temperature, supply, and device-parameter variations.
2. Bandwidth extension by $(1 + A\beta)$; GBP is preserved.
3. Reduction of nonlinear distortion and internal noise by $(1 + A\beta)$.

**Two disadvantages (1.5 marks, 0.5 each):**
1. Reduction of overall gain by $(1 + A\beta)$; may require additional gain stages to compensate.
2. Risk of oscillation if phase margin is poor; feedback amplifiers can become unstable at high frequencies.

**(b) Numerical computation:**

**Step 1 — Desensitivity factor:**

$$1 + A\beta = 1 + 2000 \times 0.04 = 1 + 80 = 81$$

**Step 2 — New midband gain:**

$$A_{f,\text{mid}} = \frac{A_{\text{mid}}}{1 + A\beta} = \frac{2000}{81} = 24.69\ \text{V/V}$$

$$A_{f,\text{mid(dB)}} = 20 \log_{10}(24.69) = 27.85\ \text{dB}$$

**Step 3 — New upper cutoff:**

$$f_{H,f} = f_H (1 + A\beta) = 25 \times 10^3 \times 81 = 2.025 \times 10^6\ \text{Hz} = 2.025\ \text{MHz}$$

**Step 4 — New lower cutoff:**

$$f_{L,f} = \frac{f_L}{1 + A\beta} = \frac{50}{81} = 0.617\ \text{Hz}$$

**Step 5 — New bandwidth:**

$$BW_f = f_{H,f} - f_{L,f} \approx 2.025\ \text{MHz} - 0.617\ \text{Hz} \approx 2.025\ \text{MHz}$$

**Step 6 — GBP invariance check:**

$$\text{GBP}_{\text{open}} = A_{\text{mid}} \cdot f_H = 2000 \times 25 \times 10^3 = 50 \times 10^6\ \text{Hz} = 50\ \text{MHz}$$

$$\text{GBP}_{\text{closed}} = A_{f,\text{mid}} \cdot f_{H,f} = 24.69 \times 2.025 \times 10^6 = 49.997 \times 10^6\ \text{Hz} \approx 50\ \text{MHz} \quad \checkmark$$

> [!TIP]
> **Verification trick:** The slight difference (49.997 vs. 50.000) is due to rounding. Students should write the answer in the form "GBP = $5 \times 10^7\ \text{Hz}$ (constant)" to earn full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this page before entering the exam hall.**

- ☐ **Universal closed-loop gain:** $A_f = A / (1 + A\beta)$.
- ☐ **Amount of feedback (desensitivity factor):** $(1+A\beta)$, *not* $A\beta$.
- ☐ **Loop gain:** $A\beta$ (dimensionless for voltage amplifiers).
- ☐ **Series mixing** → $R_{in}$ **multiplied** by $(1+A\beta)$.
- ☐ **Shunt mixing** → $R_{in}$ **divided** by $(1+A\beta)$.
- ☐ **Voltage sampling** → $R_{out}$ **divided** by $(1+A\beta)$.
- ☐ **Current sampling** → $R_{out}$ **multiplied** by $(1+A\beta)$.
- ☐ **Bandwidth** extended by $(1+A\beta)$; **GBP** conserved.
- ☐ **Lower cutoff** divided by $(1+A\beta)$; **upper cutoff** multiplied by $(1+A\beta)$.
- ☐ **CE with unbypassed $R_E$** = Current-Series Feedback; $A_f \approx -R_C / R_E$.
- ☐ **CE with C↔B $R_f$** = Voltage-Series Feedback; $A_f \approx -R_C / R_E$ after Miller transform (numerator sign depends on configuration).
- ☐ **Emitter follower** = Voltage-Series Feedback with $\beta \approx 1$, $A_f \approx 1$.
- ☐ **Negative feedback** reduces distortion, noise, and drift by factor $(1+A\beta)$.
- ☐ **Risks:** gain loss, possible oscillation, reduced slew rate.
- ☐ **Sign convention:** for negative feedback, $A$ and $\beta$ must have *opposite* signs so that $(1 + A\beta) > 1$.
- ☐ **Exam mantra:** *"Series-series raises both Rs; voltage-shunt lowers both Rs; the other two are mirror images."*

---

**End of Module Experiment 2 Notes — Feedback Amplifiers (Current Series, Voltage Series)**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Low Frequency Oscillators - Wien Bridge Oscillator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPL307 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Low Frequency Oscillators - Wien Bridge Oscillator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

The **Wien Bridge Oscillator** is a classic, low-frequency, audio-range sinusoidal signal generator that employs an **RC (Resistance-Capacitance) frequency-selective feedback network** known as the *Wien Bridge* in conjunction with a high-gain active amplifying element (typically an op-amp configured in a non-inverting topology). It generates distortion-free, low-distortion sine waves over a frequency range from approximately **1 Hz to 1 MHz**, making it the de-facto standard oscillator for audio frequency (AF) applications.

The circuit essentially operates on the principle of the **Barkhausen Criterion**, requiring:
- The closed-loop gain magnitude to be **≥ 3**
- The total phase shift around the loop to be **0° (or an integer multiple of 360°)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A Wien Bridge Oscillator is the canonical example of a *low-frequency oscillator* in the KTU Analog Electronics laboratory syllabus. Students must be able to identify the **series RC arm** and **parallel RC arm**, derive the expression for the **frequency of oscillation f₀ = 1/(2πRC)**, and explain the role of **amplitude stabilization** in producing a clean sine wave.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are pushing a child on a swing. To keep the swing moving smoothly, you must:
1. **Push at exactly the right moment** (synchronization / phase condition), AND
2. **Push with just enough force to maintain the swing's motion** — not too weak (motion dies out), not too strong (the swing flies out of control / waveform distorts).

The Wien Bridge Oscillator is the electronic equivalent of this:
- The **Wien Bridge (frequency-selective RC network)** ensures the oscillator only "amplifies" one specific frequency — the **resonant frequency** — analogous to pushing the swing at the right moment.
- The **op-amp (amplifier stage)** provides the precise energy boost needed — analogous to the push.
- **Amplitude stabilization** (using diodes, thermistors, or incandescent lamps) ensures the push is "just right" — not too strong, not too weak — yielding a clean, undistorted sine wave.

> [!NOTE]
> The frequency-determining element is purely **resistive and capacitive** (no inductors), which is why this oscillator is preferred over LC oscillators at audio frequencies. Inductors at low frequencies become impractically large and bulky.

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics
- **Frequency Range:** **1 Hz to 1 MHz** (audio range)
- **Required Loop Gain (A·β):** **≥ 1** at start-up, stable at **1** during sustained operation
- **Voltage Gain of Amplifier Stage:** **A = 3** (sustained)
- **Feedback Factor (β):** **1/3** at the resonant frequency
- **Phase Shift Through Wien Network at f₀:** **0°**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Block-Level Architecture

A Wien Bridge Oscillator has two essential building blocks:

1. **The Amplifier Stage (Active Network):**
   - Implemented using an **op-amp (e.g., μA741, TL081, LM741)** in a non-inverting configuration.
   - Provides the necessary voltage gain (theoretically 3, practically slightly > 3 at start-up).

2. **The Feedback Network (Frequency-Selective Passive Network):**
   - The **Wien Bridge** — a four-arm network consisting of:
     - **Series Arm (Z₁):** A resistor (R) in series with a capacitor (C)
     - **Parallel Arm (Z₂):** A resistor (R) in parallel with a capacitor (C)
     - Plus two purely resistive arms (R₃ and R₄ forming the gain-setting divider).

### 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

1. **Power-up Phase:** When the DC supply is switched ON, thermal noise in the circuit provides a tiny, broadband signal containing all frequencies. This noise acts as the "seed" for oscillation.

2. **Frequency Selection:** The Wien Bridge network allows only one specific frequency (the **resonant frequency f₀**) to pass through the positive feedback path with **zero phase shift** and **maximum feedback ratio (β = 1/3)**.

3. **Amplification:** The op-amp, with its voltage gain set to exactly **A = 3** (using the R_f and R_in feedback resistors), amplifies this single frequency component.

4. **Barkhausen Condition Verification:** For sustained oscillation, the **loop gain A·β = 3 × (1/3) = 1**, and the **total phase shift = 0° (or 360°)**. This is the mathematical condition for steady oscillation.

5. **Steady-State Stabilization:** At start-up, A is kept slightly **> 3** (e.g., 3.001) so the oscillation amplitude *grows* exponentially. As the amplitude rises, the **non-linear amplitude-stabilization element** (diode, thermistor, or lamp) automatically reduces the gain to exactly 3, locking the amplitude at a stable value.

### 2.3 The Role of Amplitude Stabilization

| Stabilization Method | Operating Principle | Typical Component |
|---|---|---|
| **Diode Limiter** | Diodes conduct when output exceeds a threshold, shunting excess gain | 1N4148, 1N914 |
| **Thermistor (NTC)** | As current increases, temperature rises, resistance drops, gain reduces | NTC thermistor |
| **Incandescent Lamp** | Filament resistance rises with temperature, modulating gain | Small signal lamp (e.g., 12V/50mA) |
| **JFET as VCR** | Gate voltage controlled by output amplitude, adjusting drain-source resistance | 2N3819, BF245 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Without amplitude stabilization**, the output will either die out (if A < 3) or saturate and clip into a **distorted square-wave-like waveform** (if A > 3 forever). The stabilization mechanism is therefore the "soul" of a clean Wien Bridge Oscillator.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Frequency of Oscillation (f₀)** | $$f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R C}$$ | Hz (for equal R and C in both arms) |
| 2 | **Angular Frequency (ω₀)** | $$\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{R C}$$ | rad/s |
| 3 | **Feedback Factor (β)** | $$\beta = \dfrac{1}{3}$$ | Dimensionless (at f = f₀) |
| 4 | **Required Voltage Gain (A)** | $$A = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_{in}} = 3$$ | Dimensionless |
| 5 | **Feedback Resistor Ratio** | $$\dfrac{R_f}{R_{in}} = 2$$ | Means R_f = 2·R_in |
| 6 | **Loop Gain (Barkhausen)** | $$\vert A \beta \vert \geq 1$$ | At start-up: > 1, in steady state: = 1 |
| 7 | **Total Loop Phase Shift** | $$\angle A + \angle \beta = 0^\circ \text{ or } 360^\circ$$ | Sustained oscillation condition |
| 8 | **Impedance of Series Arm (Z_s)** | $$Z_s = R - j\dfrac{1}{\omega C}$$ | Ohms (Ω) |
| 9 | **Impedance of Parallel Arm (Z_p)** | $$Z_p = \dfrac{R}{1 + j\omega R C}$$ | Ohms (Ω) |
| 10 | **Transfer Function β(jω)** | $$\beta(j\omega) = \dfrac{1}{3 + j\left(\omega RC - \dfrac{1}{\omega RC}\right)}$$ | Dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU Tip:** In the formula table, use `\vert` for magnitude signs to prevent markdown table syntax breakage.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Audio Signal Generators** (function generators used in every electronics lab).
- **Tone Generators** in musical instruments and alarm circuits.
- **Carrier Wave Generation** in low-frequency communication systems.
- **Biomedical Instrumentation** (ECG/EEG/EMG test equipment).
- **Test and Measurement Equipment** — Wien Bridge is also used as a **null-detector circuit** for measuring unknown capacitances and frequencies (the "Wien Bridge as a measuring instrument").
- **Phase-Locked Loop (PLL) voltage-controlled oscillators** (VCOs) in low-frequency synthesis.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Frequency of Oscillation f₀

**Objective:** To derive the expression $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R C}$ for a Wien Bridge Oscillator.

**Step 1 — Identify the Wien Network Configuration:**

The Wien Bridge consists of two RC arms (assuming matched components, R is the same in both arms, C is the same in both arms):

- **Series Arm:** A resistor R in series with a capacitor C. Its impedance is:

$$Z_s = R + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}$$

- **Parallel Arm:** A resistor R in parallel with a capacitor C. Its impedance is:

$$Z_p = R \parallel \dfrac{1}{j\omega C} = \dfrac{R \cdot \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}}{R + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}}$$

**Step 2 — Simplify the Parallel Arm Impedance Z_p:**

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $j\omega C$:

$$Z_p = \dfrac{R}{1 + j\omega R C}$$

**Step 3 — Write the Feedback Transfer Function β(jω):**

The feedback factor is the voltage divider ratio between the parallel arm and the total impedance (series + parallel):

$$\beta(j\omega) = \dfrac{Z_p}{Z_s + Z_p}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Z_s and Z_p:**

$$\beta(j\omega) = \dfrac{\dfrac{R}{1 + j\omega R C}}{\left(R + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}\right) + \dfrac{R}{1 + j\omega R C}}$$

**Step 5 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $(1 + j\omega RC)$:**

$$\beta(j\omega) = \dfrac{R}{\left(R + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}\right)(1 + j\omega R C) + R}$$

**Step 6 — Expand the denominator:**

The denominator becomes:

$$\left(R + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}\right)(1 + j\omega R C) + R = R(1 + j\omega R C) + \dfrac{1 + j\omega R C}{j\omega C} + R$$

$$= R + j\omega R^2 C + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C} + R + R$$

$$= 3R + j\omega R^2 C + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}$$

**Step 7 — Recognize that $\dfrac{1}{j\omega C} = -\dfrac{j}{\omega C}$:**

$$3R + j\omega R^2 C - \dfrac{j}{\omega C}$$

$$= 3R + j\left(\omega R^2 C - \dfrac{1}{\omega C}\right)$$

**Step 8 — Rewrite the feedback transfer function:**

$$\beta(j\omega) = \dfrac{R}{3R + j\left(\omega R^2 C - \dfrac{1}{\omega C}\right)} = \dfrac{1}{3 + j\left(\omega R C - \dfrac{1}{\omega R C}\right)}$$

**Step 9 — Apply the Oscillation Condition (Phase Shift = 0°):**

For the Wien network to be purely resistive (zero imaginary part, i.e., zero phase shift), the imaginary component in the denominator must vanish:

$$\omega R C - \dfrac{1}{\omega R C} = 0$$

Solving:

$$\omega R C = \dfrac{1}{\omega R C}$$

$$(\omega R C)^2 = 1$$

$$\omega R C = 1$$

$$\omega = \dfrac{1}{R C}$$

**Step 10 — Convert Angular Frequency to Frequency in Hz:**

$$f_0 = \dfrac{\omega_0}{2\pi} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R C}$$

$$\boxed{\,f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R C}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Result:** The Wien Bridge Oscillator oscillates at a frequency $f_0$ determined solely by the values of R and C. By varying R (using a ganged potentiometer — i.e., two variable resistors on a common shaft) or C, the frequency can be continuously tuned.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Gain Condition A = 3

**Objective:** To derive the required amplifier gain A for sustained oscillation.

**Step 1 — Recall the Barkhausen Criterion:**

For sustained oscillation:

$$\vert A \beta \vert = 1 \quad \text{and} \quad \angle(A\beta) = 0^\circ$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the value of β at f₀:**

At the resonant frequency $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$, the feedback factor becomes purely real:

$$\beta(j\omega_0) = \dfrac{1}{3 + j(1 - 1)} = \dfrac{1}{3}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the magnitude condition:**

$$\vert A \vert \cdot \vert \beta \vert = 1$$

$$A \cdot \dfrac{1}{3} = 1$$

$$A = 3$$

**Step 4 — Translate this into Resistor Values:**

For a non-inverting op-amp configuration:

$$A = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_{in}} = 3$$

$$\dfrac{R_f}{R_{in}} = 2$$

$$R_f = 2 R_{in}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Note:** For start-up, A is kept at **3.001 or slightly above 3** so that the oscillation amplitude *grows* from noise. As it grows, the amplitude stabilization network (diode/thermistor/lamp) automatically reduces the effective gain toward exactly 3, locking the amplitude.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Verification of Phase Shift

**Step 1 — Phase Contribution of the Amplifier (Non-Inverting):**

The non-inverting op-amp introduces a phase shift of **0°** (or 360°) between input and output.

$$\angle A = 0^\circ$$

**Step 2 — Phase Contribution of the Wien Network at f₀:**

From the transfer function:

$$\angle \beta(j\omega_0) = -\arctan\left(\dfrac{\omega_0 R C - \dfrac{1}{\omega_0 R C}}{3}\right) = -\arctan(0) = 0^\circ$$

**Step 3 — Total Phase Shift:**

$$\angle A + \angle \beta = 0^\circ + 0^\circ = 0^\circ$$

**Condition Satisfied.** Hence, oscillation is sustained.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Component Values vs Frequency (Worked Example)

Let us tabulate the frequency of oscillation for various standard R and C values, which is a frequently asked question in KTU exams:

| # | R (kΩ) | C (nF) | $f_0$ Calculation | $f_0$ (Hz) | $f_0$ (kHz) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | 1 | $1/(2\pi \cdot 10^3 \cdot 10^{-9})$ | 159,154.9 | 159.15 |
| 2 | 10 | 1 | $1/(2\pi \cdot 10^4 \cdot 10^{-9})$ | 15,915.5 | 15.92 |
| 3 | 10 | 10 | $1/(2\pi \cdot 10^4 \cdot 10^{-8})$ | 1,591.5 | 1.59 |
| 4 | 100 | 10 | $1/(2\pi \cdot 10^5 \cdot 10^{-8})$ | 159.2 | 0.159 |
| 5 | 1,000 | 100 | $1/(2\pi \cdot 10^6 \cdot 10^{-7})$ | 1.59 | 0.0016 |

> [!NOTE]
> Notice how doubling R halves the frequency, and doubling C also halves the frequency. The product R·C is the "time constant" that determines the period of oscillation.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of the Wien Bridge Oscillator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DC["DC Power Supply Rails (+Vcc and -Vee)"]
        PSUP[Power Supply]
    end

    subgraph ACTIVE["Active Amplifier Stage - Op-Amp 741"]
        OA[Operational Amplifier\nNon-Inverting Configuration\nGain A = 3]
        RFB[Feedback Resistor Rf\nSets Gain]
        RIN[Input Resistor Rin]
    end

    subgraph PASSIVE["Wien Bridge Frequency Selective Network"]
        ZS[Series RC Arm\nZs = R + 1 over j omega C]
        ZP[Parallel RC Arm\nZp = R parallel to 1 over j omega C]
    end

    subgraph STABILIZER["Amplitude Stabilization Block"]
        STAB[Diode Limiter or\nThermistor or\nIncandescent Lamp]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["Output Stage"]
        OUT[Sine Wave Output\nf0 = 1 over 2 pi RC]
        LOAD[Load / CRO / Speaker]
    end

    NOISE[Thermal Noise Source\nInitial Seed Signal] --> ZS
    ZS --> ZP
    ZP -->|Positive Feedback| OA
    OA -->|Output Vo| OUT
    OUT --> LOAD
    OUT --> STAB
    STAB -->|Gain Control Signal| RFB
    RIN --> OA
    PSUP --> OA
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram is a **functional block-level architecture** rather than a literal circuit schematic. The thermal noise (initial seed) is filtered by the Wien network, amplified by the op-amp, and the output amplitude is monitored and stabilized by the amplitude stabilization block, which dynamically adjusts the gain to exactly 3 in steady state.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Signal Flow & Feedback Topology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INPUT[Input Node\nV_in] --> SUM[Summing Junction]
    SUM --> AMP[Amplifier\nGain A = 3]
    AMP --> VOUT[Output Node\nV_out]
    VOUT --> FB1[Feedback Network Beta j omega\nWien Bridge]
    FB1 --> SUM

    VOUT --> AMPSTAB[Amplitude Stabilizer]
    AMPSTAB -.->|Modulates A| AMP

    NOTE1[Phase Condition: angle A + angle Beta = 0 deg] -.-> SUM
    NOTE2[Gain Condition: A times Beta = 1] -.-> AMP
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Barkhausen Criterion Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    NOISESRC[Broadband Thermal Noise\nV_n at all frequencies] --> WIENFILTER[Wien Bridge Filter\nPasses only f0]
    WIENFILTER -->|Single frequency f0| AMPGAIN[Amplifier\nGain A = 3]
    AMPGAIN --> OSCILLATING[Growing Sine Wave\nat f0]
    OSCILLATING --> FEEDBACK[Feedback via Beta = 1 over 3]
    FEEDBACK --> WIENFILTER

    OSCILLATING --> STABN[Stabilization\nA settles to 3]
    STABN -->|Steady state| STEADY[Stable Sine Wave Output]

    style NOISESRC fill:#FFE4B5
    style WIENFILTER fill:#B0E0E6
    style AMPGAIN fill:#98FB98
    style STEADY fill:#FFD700
```

---

### 4.4 Physical Circuit Topology (Descriptive Schematic)

The standard Wien Bridge Oscillator circuit has the following node-level topology:

- **Non-Inverting Input (+)** of op-amp is connected to the **junction of the series arm (Z_s) and the parallel arm (Z_p)**.
- **Inverting Input (−)** of op-amp is connected to the **voltage divider formed by R_f and R_in**.
- **Op-amp output** feeds back simultaneously to:
  - The **start of the series arm Z_s** (positive feedback path through the Wien network).
  - The **top of R_f** (negative feedback path for gain setting).

The component values typically chosen:
- **R = 10 kΩ**, **C = 0.01 μF** → $f_0 = 1/(2\pi \cdot 10^4 \cdot 10^{-8}) \approx 1.59 \text{ kHz}$
- **R_f ≈ 20 kΩ**, **R_in ≈ 10 kΩ** → Gain A = 3

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define a Wien Bridge Oscillator. State the expression for the frequency of oscillation and mention its typical frequency range.*

**Model Answer:**

A Wien Bridge Oscillator is a low-frequency sinusoidal oscillator that uses a **Wien Bridge** (a frequency-selective RC network consisting of a series RC arm and a parallel RC arm) as the positive feedback element and an op-amp as the active amplifying element.

**Frequency of oscillation:**

$$f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R C}$$

**Typical frequency range:** **1 Hz to 1 MHz** (audio range).

> *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for frequency range)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*State the Barkhausen criterion for sustained oscillation. Apply it to a Wien Bridge Oscillator and find the required gain and feedback factor.*

**Model Answer:**

The **Barkhausen Criterion** states that for sustained sinusoidal oscillation:
1. The magnitude of the loop gain must equal unity: $\vert A \beta \vert = 1$
2. The total phase shift around the loop must be **0° (or 360°)**: $\angle A + \angle \beta = 0^\circ$

**For a Wien Bridge Oscillator:**
- At the resonant frequency, the Wien network is purely resistive, so $\beta = \dfrac{1}{3}$ (real and positive).
- For $\vert A \beta \vert = 1$, we require $A = 3$.
- Hence, the **required voltage gain is 3** and the **feedback factor is 1/3**.

> *(3 marks: 1 for criterion, 1 for β, 1 for A)*

---

### Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] — Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the frequency of oscillation of a Wien Bridge Oscillator using an op-amp. Show that at resonance, the feedback factor $\beta = 1/3$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a Wien Bridge Oscillator. What is the role of amplitude stabilization? Mention two methods of amplitude stabilization. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation of f₀ and β = 1/3 (7 marks):**

Refer to the **Derivation 1** and **Derivation 2** in Section 3 of these notes for the full step-by-step solution.

**Summary of derivation steps:**

1. Write the impedances of the series and parallel arms:

$$Z_s = R + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}, \quad Z_p = \dfrac{R}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

2. Form the feedback transfer function:

$$\beta(j\omega) = \dfrac{Z_p}{Z_s + Z_p} = \dfrac{1}{3 + j\left(\omega RC - \dfrac{1}{\omega RC}\right)}$$

3. Apply the **zero phase condition** (imaginary part = 0):

$$\omega R C = \dfrac{1}{\omega R C} \Rightarrow \omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{RC}$$

4. Substitute back to get $\beta$ at resonance:

$$\beta(j\omega_0) = \dfrac{1}{3 + j(1-1)} = \dfrac{1}{3}$$

5. Final expression for frequency of oscillation:

$$f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$$

**(b) Block Diagram and Amplitude Stabilization (7 marks):**

- **Block diagram:** Refer to the **Mermaid Block Diagram** in Section 4.1 of these notes. Describe the flow: noise source → Wien Bridge filter → op-amp (gain A=3) → output → feedback through Wien Bridge.
- **Working:** Thermal noise provides the seed signal. The Wien Bridge passes only the resonant frequency f₀. The op-amp amplifies with gain A=3. Output is fed back positively. As amplitude grows, the stabilization network adjusts A to keep output constant.
- **Role of amplitude stabilization:** Prevents the output from either dying out (if A < 3) or saturating into a distorted waveform (if A > 3 indefinitely). It ensures a clean, stable sine wave at a constant amplitude.
- **Two methods of amplitude stabilization:**
  1. **Diode-based limiter** (back-to-back Zener diodes or signal diodes across the gain-setting resistor)
  2. **Thermistor-based stabilization** (NTC thermistor in place of R_f; resistance decreases as temperature rises, reducing gain)
  3. **(Alternative)** Incandescent lamp in place of R_f; JFET as a voltage-controlled resistor.

> *(Mark Split: 7 = 4 for derivation, 3 for diagram/working/stabilization)*

---

**Q4. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] — Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** Explain the operation of a Wien Bridge Oscillator with a circuit diagram. State and explain the Barkhausen criterion. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a Wien Bridge Oscillator with R = 10 kΩ and C = 0.01 μF in both arms, calculate: (i) the frequency of oscillation, (ii) the feedback factor at resonance, (iii) the required amplifier gain. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Operation and Barkhausen Criterion (7 marks):**

Refer to Sections 2.2 and 2.3 for the complete theory.

- The Wien Bridge Oscillator uses a non-inverting op-amp with the Wien Bridge in the positive feedback path. The Wien Bridge consists of a series RC arm and a parallel RC arm, both with the same R and C values.
- At the resonant frequency, the Wien network acts as a purely resistive voltage divider, providing a feedback ratio of 1/3 with zero phase shift.
- The op-amp is configured with a feedback resistor network (R_f and R_in) to set the voltage gain to 3. The non-inverting topology ensures zero phase shift.
- **Barkhausen Criterion:** Two conditions for sustained oscillation: (1) Loop gain $\vert A \beta \vert = 1$ and (2) Loop phase shift = 0° (or 360°). Both are satisfied by the Wien Bridge Oscillator at $f_0 = 1/(2\pi RC)$.

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:** R = 10 kΩ = $10 \times 10^3$ Ω, C = 0.01 μF = $0.01 \times 10^{-6}$ F = $10^{-8}$ F.

**(i) Frequency of oscillation:**

$$f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R C} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \cdot 10^4 \cdot 10^{-8}}$$

$$f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \cdot 10^{-4}} = \dfrac{10^4}{2\pi}$$

$$f_0 = \dfrac{10{,}000}{6.2832} \approx 1591.55 \text{ Hz}$$

$$\boxed{f_0 \approx 1.591 \text{ kHz}}$$

**(ii) Feedback factor at resonance:**

$$\beta = \dfrac{1}{3} \approx 0.333$$

**(iii) Required amplifier gain:**

$$A = 3$$

To achieve this with a non-inverting op-amp, $R_f = 2 R_{in}$. For example, $R_{in} = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $R_f = 20 \text{ k}\Omega$.

> *(Mark Split: 7 = 2 for f₀ setup, 2 for f₀ numerical answer, 1 for β, 2 for A and resistor values)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse the gain condition for start-up vs steady state.** Many students write "A = 3" without mentioning that **at start-up, A must be slightly greater than 3** for oscillations to build up. A precisely equals 3 only in **steady state** due to the amplitude stabilization network. **Lose 1 mark** if you do not mention this distinction.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention the zero-phase condition** is a common mistake. The Barkhausen criterion has *two* parts — magnitude AND phase. Both must be stated. **Lose 1 mark** if you state only the magnitude condition.
> 3. **Unit conversion errors** in numerical problems (kΩ vs Ω, μF vs F, nF vs F) are the most common source of lost marks. Always convert to **base SI units (Ω and F)** before substituting into the formula.
> 4. **Wrong identification of which RC arm is series vs parallel** in the circuit diagram. The **series arm** has R and C in series (one node in common — the junction); the **parallel arm** has R and C in parallel (two nodes in common). Be precise.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Wien Bridge Oscillator = RC oscillator** for low/audio frequencies (1 Hz to 1 MHz). No inductors used.
- ✅ **Wien Network** = Series RC arm (Z_s) + Parallel RC arm (Z_p), both with equal R and C.
- ✅ **Frequency of oscillation:** $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ — purely determined by R and C values.
- ✅ **At resonance:** $\beta = \dfrac{1}{3}$ (purely real, zero phase shift).
- ✅ **Required gain:** $A = 3$ (steady state); **A > 3** at start-up.
- ✅ **Barkhausen Criterion:** $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ AND $\angle(A\beta) = 0°$.
- ✅ **Amplitude stabilization** is critical to prevent the output from saturating (clipping) or dying out.
- ✅ **Methods of amplitude stabilization:** Diode limiter, NTC thermistor, incandescent lamp, JFET voltage-controlled resistor.
- ✅ **Active element:** Op-amp (μA741, TL081, LM741) in **non-inverting** configuration.
- ✅ **Practical application:** Audio function generators, tone generators, biomedical instruments.
- ✅ **Frequency tuning:** Use a **ganged potentiometer** (two variable resistors on a common shaft) to vary R and change f₀ continuously.
- ✅ **Common KTU exam tip:** Numerical problems usually give R in kΩ and C in nF or μF — convert to base units (Ω, F) before calculating.
- ✅ **Phase check:** Non-inverting op-amp contributes **0°**; Wien network at f₀ contributes **0°**; total = **0°** ✓.
- ✅ **Quality factor** of the Wien network at resonance: $Q = \dfrac{1}{3}$ (moderately selective).
- ✅ **No inductors** means no magnetic interference, no bulky components, and **easy integration** in IC form.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Mantra for Wien Bridge Oscillator:**
> *"RC Network, f₀ = 1/(2πRC), β = 1/3, A = 3, Phase = 0°, Stabilize the Amplitude, No Inductors, Audio Range."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Operational Amplifiers Basics - Measurement of Opamp Parameters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPL307 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Operational Amplifiers Basics - Measurement of Opamp Parameters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 The Operational Amplifier — Formal Definition

An **Operational Amplifier (Op-Amp)** is a high-gain, direct-coupled, differential-input, single-ended-output DC amplifier whose gain, input impedance, output impedance, and bandwidth can be precisely controlled through external feedback networks. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context for **PCCPL307**, the canonical laboratory device studied is the **µA741** (or equivalent such as LM741, TL081), implemented in an 8-pin DIP package.

Mathematically, the ideal open-loop output voltage is expressed as:

$$V_{out} = A_{OL} \cdot (V^{+} - V^{-})$$

where $A_{OL}$ is the open-loop differential voltage gain (typically $10^5$ to $10^6$ for the 741), $V^{+}$ is the non-inverting input, and $V^{-}$ is the inverting input.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCCPL307 — Module 2):**
> The lab experiment is explicitly titled *"Operational Amplifiers Basics — Measurement of Opamp Parameters."* Students are expected to *measure* (not merely define) the following DC and AC parameters using standard test circuits:
> 1. Input Bias Current ($I_{B}$)
> 2. Input Offset Current ($I_{IO}$)
> 3. Input Offset Voltage ($V_{IO}$)
> 4. Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)
> 5. Slew Rate (SR)
> 6. Open-Loop Voltage Gain ($A_{OL}$)
> 7. Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "See-Saw With Imperfections" Model

Imagine a perfectly balanced **see-saw** with two seats. An ideal op-amp is a *perfect* see-saw: push down on one side by 1 mm, the other side rises by an enormous amount (the "gain"), and the pivot is frictionless (infinite input impedance). 

Now consider the *real* see-saw on a playground:
- **The pivot is slightly rusty** → a small current ($I_B$) must be supplied to the inputs to make it move at all.
- **The two arms are of slightly unequal length** → a small *voltage* imbalance ($V_{IO}$) is needed to level it.
- **A storm shakes the entire playground equally from side to side** → the see-saw jiggles up and down together (common-mode noise), and the *Common-Mode Rejection Ratio* tells us how well the see-saw ignores this.
- **A child jumps on too violently** → the see-saw cannot rise fast enough (this is the **slew rate limit**).

Every "imperfection" of a real op-amp is precisely one of the parameters we will measure in this experiment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Parameter Measurement Matters:**
> In real PCB design (e.g., a KTU student building an instrumentation amplifier for a biomedical sensor), an uncompensated $V_{IO}$ of 5 mV at a closed-loop gain of 1000 produces a **5 V output error**, which can saturate the ADC. Hence, *quantifying* these parameters is not academic — it is the basis of analog design reliability.

## 1.3 The 741 Op-Amp Pinout (DIP-8 Reference)

| Pin # | Name | Function |
|:---:|:---:|:---|
| 1 | **Offset Null** | Used with a 10 kΩ potentiometer to null $V_{IO}$ |
| 2 | **Inverting Input ($V^-$)** | Signal input whose output is 180° out of phase |
| 3 | **Non-Inverting Input ($V^+$)** | Signal input whose output is in phase |
| 4 | **$V^-$ Supply ($-V_{EE}$ or GND)** | Negative power rail (typically $-12$ V or $-15$ V) |
| 5 | **Offset Null** | Companion to Pin 1 |
| 6 | **Output** | Amplified output signal |
| 7 | **$V^+$ Supply ($+V_{CC}$)** | Positive power rail (typically $+12$ V or $+15$ V) |
| 8 | **NC** | No internal connection |

> [!TIP]
> **Lab Tip:** Always power the 741 with **symmetrical dual supplies** ($\pm 12$ V or $\pm 15$ V) for AC experiments. Single-supply operation requires biasing tricks and is *not* standard in KTU lab manuals.

## 1.4 Categories of Op-Amp Parameters

Parameters are classified into three functional families for clarity:

- **DC Input Parameters** — $I_{B}$, $I_{IO}$, $V_{IO}$
- **DC Transfer Parameters** — $A_{OL}$, CMRR, PSRR (Power Supply Rejection Ratio)
- **AC Dynamic Parameters** — Slew Rate (SR), Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW), Settling Time

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Input Bias Current ($I_B$)

### Conceptual Explanation
The input stage of a 741 uses **BJT Darlington pairs** (or JFET/MOSFET in other op-amps). The bases of these input transistors always require a small DC bias current to remain properly biased in the active region. This is the **input bias current**, defined as the *average* of the two input currents:

$$I_{B} = \frac{I_{B}^{+} + I_{B}^{-}}{2}$$

For the µA741, typical $I_B \approx 80$ nA (max 500 nA). For JFET-input op-amps (e.g., TL081), $I_B$ drops to **pico-amperes** (60 pA typical).

### Why It Matters
When $I_B$ flows through the *source resistance* seen by each input, it generates an unwanted DC offset voltage at the output. This is why we always use **equal source resistors** at the two inputs in precision circuits.

## 2.2 Input Offset Current ($I_{IO}$)

This is the *difference* between the two input bias currents, caused by mismatch in the input transistor pair:

$$I_{IO} = \vert I_{B}^{+} - I_{B}^{-} \vert$$

For the 741, typical $I_{IO} \approx 20$ nA. Notice $I_{IO} \ll I_B$, which reflects good transistor matching at the silicon die level.

## 2.3 Input Offset Voltage ($V_{IO}$)

Even with both inputs *exactly grounded* ($V^+ = V^- = 0$), a real op-amp produces a non-zero DC output. To drive the output to exactly 0 V, a small differential voltage must be applied — this is $V_{IO}$:

$$V_{IO} = \frac{V_{out}}{A_{CL}} \bigg\vert_{V^{+}=V^{-}=0}$$

For 741: typical $|V_{IO}| \approx 2$ mV, maximum 6 mV. Precision op-amps like OP-07 achieve $V_{IO} \approx 25$ µV.

## 2.4 Open-Loop Voltage Gain ($A_{OL}$)

The differential gain from input to output **with no feedback** applied. Expressed in V/V or in dB:

$$A_{OL(dB)} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in,diff}}\right)$$

For 741: $A_{OL} \approx 2 \times 10^5$ V/V, i.e., **106 dB** at DC. Note that $A_{OL}$ is **frequency-dependent** and rolls off at $-20$ dB/decade after a dominant pole near **10 Hz**.

## 2.5 Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)

When a signal is applied *simultaneously and equally* to both inputs (a *common-mode* signal $V_{CM}$), an ideal op-amp would completely reject it. In reality, a small output appears. The CMRR quantifies this rejection:

$$\text{CMRR} = \frac{A_{d}}{A_{cm}} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A_{d}}{A_{cm}}\right) \text{ dB}$$

where $A_d$ is the differential-mode gain and $A_{cm}$ is the common-mode gain. For 741: CMRR $\approx 90$ dB.

The common-mode input range is limited to $V_{CM} \in [V_{EE}+3\text{ V},\ V_{CC}-3\text{ V}]$ for the 741.

## 2.6 Slew Rate (SR)

The *maximum rate of change* of the output voltage in response to a large step input. It is governed by the internal **compensation capacitor** $C_C \approx 30$ pF inside the 741 and the bias current of the gain stage:

$$\text{SR} = \left.\frac{dV_{out}}{dt}\right|_{max}$$

For 741: $\text{SR} \approx 0.5\ \text{V/µs}$. Modern op-amps like LF356 achieve 12 V/µs, and current-feedback op-amps exceed 1000 V/µs.

The slew rate sets the **maximum undistorted sine-wave frequency**:

$$f_{max} = \frac{\text{SR}}{2\pi V_{p}}$$

## 2.7 Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW)

The constant product of closed-loop gain and the corresponding $-3$ dB bandwidth — a fundamental figure of merit of an op-amp:

$$\text{GBW} = A_{CL} \times f_{-3dB} = \text{constant} \approx f_{T}$$

For 741: GBW $\approx 1$ MHz. For TL081: GBW $\approx 3$ MHz. For OP-27: GBW $\approx 8$ MHz.

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Symbol | Formula / Definition | Typical 741 Value | Unit |
|:-:|:----------|:------:|:---------------------|:------------------|:----:|
| 1 | Input Bias Current | $I_B$ | $(I_B^{+} + I_B^{-})/2$ | 80 | nA |
| 2 | Input Offset Current | $I_{IO}$ | $\vert I_B^{+} - I_B^{-} \vert$ | 20 | nA |
| 3 | Input Offset Voltage | $V_{IO}$ | $V_{out}/A_{CL}$ at $V^+=V^-=0$ | 2 | mV |
| 4 | Open-Loop Gain | $A_{OL}$ | $20\log_{10}(V_{out}/V_{in,diff})$ | 106 | dB |
| 5 | CMRR | CMRR | $20\log_{10}(A_d/A_{cm})$ | 90 | dB |
| 6 | Slew Rate | SR | $dV_{out}/dt \big\vert_{max}$ | 0.5 | V/µs |
| 7 | GBW | GBW | $A_{CL} \cdot f_{-3dB}$ | 1 | MHz |
| 8 | Max Sine Frequency | $f_{max}$ | $\text{SR} / (2\pi V_p)$ | computed | Hz |
| 9 | Output Error due to $V_{IO}$ | $V_{out,err}$ | $V_{IO} \cdot A_{CL}$ | variable | V |
| 10 | Power Supply Rejection | PSRR | $20\log_{10}(\Delta V_{CC}/V_{IO})$ | 90 | dB |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Absolute Value Convention:** Throughout the KTU Formula Sheet, all magnitude expressions use the symbols $\vert \cdot \vert$ rather than the vertical pipe `|`, to maintain markdown table integrity. In LaTeX-rendered documents, both forms are equivalent.

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Instrumentation Amplifiers (ECG/EEG front-ends):** CMRR $> 100$ dB is mandatory to reject 50/60 Hz mains hum picked up by the body.
- **Audio Preamplifiers:** Low $V_{IO}$ and low $I_B$ are critical; otherwise, DC offset at the output can damage loudspeakers.
- **Sample-and-Hold Circuits in ADCs:** Slew rate determines the **acquisition time** for a given voltage step.
- **Active Filters:** GBW must be at least $50\times$ the filter cutoff frequency to avoid gain peaking and Q-errors.
- **Voltage Regulators & Precision Rectifiers:** All seven parameters above are simultaneously relevant.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of Output Error Due to Input Offset Voltage

We derive the DC output error when a real op-amp with finite $V_{IO}$ is placed in a non-inverting amplifier configuration with gain $A_{CL} = 1 + R_f/R_{in}$.

**Step 1 — Define the configuration.**

The non-inverting amplifier has the input $V_{in}$ applied to $V^+$, while $V^-$ is tied to the junction of $R_{in}$ and $R_f$ (feedback network).

**Step 2 — Write the input-output relationship for an ideal op-amp.**

For an ideal op-amp with infinite $A_{OL}$, we have $V_{out} = A_{CL} \cdot V_{in}$.

**Step 3 — Introduce the offset voltage.**

The internal $V_{IO}$ appears as a small differential source between $V^+$ and $V^-$. The governing equation becomes:

$$V_{out} = A_{OL}\bigl[(V_{in}) - (V_{out}/A_{CL}) + V_{IO}\bigr]$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $V_{out}$.**

Rearranging:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{out} &= A_{OL} \cdot V_{in} - A_{OL} \cdot \frac{V_{out}}{A_{CL}} + A_{OL} \cdot V_{IO} \\
V_{out} + A_{OL} \cdot \frac{V_{out}}{A_{CL}} &= A_{OL} \cdot V_{in} + A_{OL} \cdot V_{IO} \\
V_{out} \left(1 + \frac{A_{OL}}{A_{CL}}\right) &= A_{OL}(V_{in} + V_{IO}) \\
V_{out} &= \frac{A_{OL}}{1 + A_{OL}/A_{CL}} (V_{in} + V_{IO})
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Take the realistic limit.**

Since $A_{OL}/A_{CL} \gg 1$ (for the 741, $2 \times 10^5 / 10 = 2 \times 10^4$), the "1" in the denominator is negligible:

$$V_{out} \approx A_{CL} (V_{in} + V_{IO})$$

**Step 6 — Isolate the error term.**

With $V_{in} = 0$ (grounded input) for the measurement:

$$V_{out} = A_{CL} \cdot V_{IO}$$

**Conclusion:** The output DC offset is *amplified by the closed-loop gain*. Hence, the $V_{IO}$ measurement procedure is to set $V_{in}=0$, measure $V_{out}$, and divide by $A_{CL}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Slew Rate Equation

**Step 1 — Internal model.**

Inside the op-amp, the compensation capacitor $C_C$ is charged by a constant current source $I$ (Miller integrator). The output stage is a voltage follower on this capacitor.

**Step 2 — Capacitor charging relation.**

$$I = C_C \cdot \frac{dV_{out}}{dt}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the time-derivative.**

$$\frac{dV_{out}}{dt} = \frac{I}{C_C}$$

**Step 4 — Recognize that $I$ is internally limited.**

For the 741, the biasing current $I$ of the second stage is set by internal current mirrors to approximately **15 µA**, and $C_C \approx 30$ pF.

**Step 5 — Substitute and compute.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{SR} &= \frac{I}{C_C} \\
&= \frac{15 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{A}}{30 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{F}} \\
&= 0.5 \times 10^{6}\ \text{V/s} \\
&= 0.5\ \text{V/µs}
\end{aligned}$$

This matches the datasheet value, validating the model.

**Step 6 — Derive the maximum undistorted sine-wave frequency.**

For a sine wave $V_{out}(t) = V_p \sin(2\pi f t)$:

$$\frac{dV_{out}}{dt} = 2\pi f V_p \cos(2\pi f t)$$

The maximum slope occurs at the zero crossings ($t=0$):

$$\left.\frac{dV_{out}}{dt}\right|_{max} = 2\pi f V_p$$

Setting this equal to SR:

$$f_{max} = \frac{\text{SR}}{2\pi V_p}$$

For 741 with $V_p = 10$ V:

$$f_{max} = \frac{0.5 \times 10^6}{2\pi \times 10} \approx 7.96\ \text{kHz}$$

Above 8 kHz, a 10 V sine wave from the 741 will become a **triangle wave** (slew-limited).

---

## 3.3 Derivation of CMRR from Measurable Quantities

**Step 1 — Apply a common-mode signal $V_{CM}$.**

Tie both $V^+$ and $V^-$ to a single source $V_{CM}$ (with the feedback configured to hold the op-amp in linear operation). Measure the resulting $V_{out,cm}$.

**Step 2 — Compute the common-mode gain.**

$$A_{cm} = \frac{V_{out,cm}}{V_{CM}}$$

**Step 3 — Apply a differential signal $V_d$.**

Apply $V_d$ to one input and $-V_d$ to the other; measure $V_{out,d}$ and compute:

$$A_d = \frac{V_{out,d}}{V_d}$$

**Step 4 — Form the ratio.**

$$\text{CMRR} = \frac{A_d}{A_{cm}} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right) \text{ dB}$$

**Step 5 — Practical shortcut using single measurement.**

In the KTU lab, the $A_d$ is the *closed-loop gain* (set by the feedback network, e.g., $A_{CL}=10$ for a non-inverting amp), so:
